target.title: docs(gateway): document high-memory local Ollama compaction tuning
target.body:
﻿Fixes #55888

## What Problem This Solves

High-memory local Ollama users have existing compaction knobs that can keep more recent transcript in large-context sessions, but the local models guide did not explain the supported profile, threshold math, or safe verification path. Users had to infer why a 32k context model started memory flush or preflight compaction earlier than expected.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a focused docs-only profile to the local models guide for 32 GB / 64 GB+ Ollama setups. It documents the existing `agents.defaults.compaction` keys, explains the `contextWindow - reserveTokensFloor - memoryFlush.softThresholdTokens` threshold calculation, and keeps runtime defaults unchanged for lower-memory machines.

## User Impact

Users running larger local Ollama contexts can now tune compaction intentionally, see the expected 32,768 - 2,000 - 4,000 = 26,768 threshold example, and confirm the effective `contextWindow` and `threshold` in verbose Gateway logs before relying on a long session.

## Evidence

AI-assisted: yes. I used Codex to inspect the issue, source-backed threshold behavior, docs rules, and validation output. I reviewed the final diff and understand the change.

Validation run locally:

```text
git diff --check
# passed

node scripts/docs-list.js
# passed

node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs README.md
# Docs MDX check passed (684 files, 9324ms).

node scripts/docs-link-audit.mjs
# checked_internal_links=5464, broken_links=0

node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check
# failed on pre-existing docs/reference/templates/CLAUDE.md, not touched by this PR
```

Attempted the requested pnpm docs commands too, but local pnpm ran a dependency status/install path before the scripts and failed on Windows `EPERM` renames inside `node_modules` (`@types/node` / `@lit/context`). The equivalent Node docs scripts above were run directly and passed for the changed docs surface.

target.title: fix(agents): load bootstrap files from agentDir alongside workspace
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` loads bootstrap files from `workspaceDir` only and does not read explicitly configured `agentDir`. When users configure an `agentDir` for their agents, bootstrap files placed there are ignored.

Fixes #29387

## Root Cause

`loadBootstrapFiles()` only queries `workspaceDir` and the `agentDir` config path was never passed into the loader.

## Fix

Modify `loadBootstrapFiles()` to also load explicitly configured `agentDir` bootstrap files, with `agentDir` files overriding same-named workspace files. Gate loading on explicit `agentDir` config to avoid unnecessary I/O when `agentDir` is not configured. Also filters `BOOTSTRAP.md` against the agent name filter.

## Evidence

### Test Results (vitest 4.1.8)

```
✓ src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts (2 test files, 74 tests, 100% pass)
```

All 74 bootstrap-file tests pass, covering: workspace-only bootstrap loading, agentDir bootstrap with override, explicit agentDir config gating, BOOTSTRAP.md agent name filtering, and no-config fallback.

### Real Behavior Proof
- **Behavior addressed**: Bootstrap files from `agentDir` are ignored even when explicitly configured
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node 22.22.0, vitest 4.1.8
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 2 test files, 74 tests pass, 0 failures
- **Observed result after fix**: `agentDir` bootstrap files are loaded and override workspace files when `agentDir` is explicitly configured
- **What was not tested**: Live agent bootstrap with filesystem I/O (tests use mock FS)

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I ran the circular check locally, ran the report generator locally, and inspected the generated markdown/JSON/text artifacts for sorting, counts, and representative SCC output.
- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not yet verify the rendered GitHub Actions step summary or artifact download UX inside a live PR run before opening this PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The report could be noisy or too broad for reviewers if the summary is not distilled enough.
  - Mitigation: The PR summary shows only top scopes/directories/SCCs, while the full raw listing stays in artifacts.
- Risk: The report job adds CI time.
  - Mitigation: It is non-blocking, uses a focused config, and runs in the same lightweight Node environment as other CI checks.

target.title: fix(cron): guard task liveness during startup
target.body:
## Summary

Task registry maintenance now treats cron active-job liveness as non-authoritative until cron startup reconciliation exits. This prevents a pre-start maintenance sweep from marking persisted cron task records lost while the process-local active-job set is still empty after restart.

The change keeps the boundary narrow:

- `activeJobIds` starts non-authoritative after process boot
- `cron.start()` marks liveness reconciling at entry and authoritative in `finally`
- task maintenance preserves only mapped cron tasks while cron liveness is non-authoritative
- once cron start exits, missing active-job state is trusted again

This is AI-assisted.

## Proof

Real setup: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw`, branch rebased onto `origin/main` at `435e1a74fa`.

Commands run after the rebase/conflict resolution:

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
pnpm exec vitest run src/cron/service/ops.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
```

Observed result:

- `git diff --check`: passed
- core tsgo: passed
- `src/cron/service/ops.test.ts`: 13 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts`: 21 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`: 63 passed

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Task registry maintenance should preserve mapped stale cron task records while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative during startup, then resume marking missing mapped cron tasks `lost` after cron startup exits and liveness is authoritative.

**Real setup tested:** Local `openclaw/openclaw` checkout on the PR branch, using an isolated temporary state root under `/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-*` and the repository's TypeScript runtime via `node --import tsx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx .artifacts/pr-80707-real-proof.mjs
```

The script seeds 10-minute-stale mapped cron task records with `runtime: cron` and `sourceId`, runs task maintenance once while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative, then runs task maintenance again after liveness is marked authoritative.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the isolated local state-dir run:

```json
{
  "stateRoot": "/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-s9f07s",
  "scenario": "mapped stale cron task maintenance across cron startup liveness authority",
  "staleAgeMs": 600000,
  "beforeStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": false,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 0,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-before-startup-exit",
      "status": "running",
      "endedAtPresent": false
    }
  },
  "afterStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": true,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 2,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-after-startup-exit",
      "status": "lost",
      "endedAtPresent": true,
      "error": "backing session missing"
    }
  }
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The pre-start/non-authoritative maintenance pass left the stale mapped cron task `running` with `reconciled: 0`. The post-start/authoritative maintenance pass marked the stale mapped cron task `lost` with `error: "backing session missing"`.

**Not tested:** I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

This shows the fixed behavior: task maintenance does not mark a mapped stale cron task lost during the pre-start/non-authoritative window, and does mark mapped stale cron tasks lost after startup liveness becomes authoritative again.

Also ran a focused Codex review against the rebased diff; it reported no blocker. I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

target.title: refactor(auto-reply): drop dead /reset branches in fast-path session init
target.body:
## Summary

This PR was originally proposed as a fix for #80377, claiming `/reset` was preserving session-level overrides incorrectly. After review by `clawsweeper` and inspection of `initSessionState` (the actual production reset path), it turns out the runtime behavior is **intentional**:

- behavior overrides (thinking, verbose, reasoning, ttsAuto) are deliberately preserved across `/reset` so users don't re-enable them every time (see `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts:510-518`)
- model/provider/auth overrides go through `resolveResetPreservedSelection`, which preserves user-driven selections and clears only auto-created ones (per #69301)

So this PR has been **pivoted from a behavior fix to a cleanup-only refactor**. The original commit (`1a1685a`) and the regression tests it added have been reverted in `1d70235`. The only remaining net change versus `origin/main` is replacing nine trivial `resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.X : existingEntry?.X` ternaries in `initFastReplySessionState` with the simple `existingEntry?.X` expression.

**Net effect: zero semantic change vs `origin/main`.** The dead `resetTriggered` branches no longer mislead readers into thinking the fast-path bootstrap has reset-specific logic that the runtime doesn't actually have.

Issue #80377 should be closed as wontfix / by-design (the bundle pattern that was reported is a copy-paste artifact in this fast-path init, not a production runtime bug). This PR does **not** fix #80377; it only references it.

## Files touched

- `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-fast-path.ts` — simplify 9 no-op ternaries (no semantic change)

## Behavior proof (no-op refactor)

This is a semantic no-op cleanup; the proof is the diff itself. Before/after, every assignment evaluates to the same value:

```ts
// Before (one of nine identical patterns)
thinkingLevel: resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.thinkingLevel : existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
// After
thinkingLevel: existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
```

For both `resetTriggered === true` and `resetTriggered === false`, both expressions yield `existingEntry?.thinkingLevel`. The `resetTriggered` semantics still flow through the `...(!resetTriggered ? existingEntry : undefined)` spread on line 245 (unchanged), so reset still drops `responseUsage` and `sessionStartedAt` correctly. The full pre-existing test suite for `get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` (12 tests) continues to pass:

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  12 passed (12)
```

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` → 12/12 (pre-existing tests, unchanged)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` → green
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core:test` → green (with `GOMEMLIMIT=2GiB` on a memory-constrained dev box)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: preserve plugin hook and tool closure state
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes plugin tool resolution so cached descriptor wrappers do not bypass an already-live plugin registry that owns both hooks and tools for the same plugin. That keeps hook handlers and tool factories on the same `register()` closure when a plugin uses closure state across `before_prompt_build` and a tool like `memory_add`.

## Changes

- Detect active/pinned-channel registries that already contain a plugin's tools and hooks.
- Skip cached descriptor wrappers for those stateful hook+tool plugins, forcing the normal live-registry materialization path.
- Preserve descriptor caching for plain tool-only plugins and cold/no-live-registry paths.
- Add regression coverage for a warmed descriptor cache being bypassed when a live hook+tool registry is available.

## Testing

- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" pnpm vitest run src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` — passed, 59 tests.
- `git diff --check` — passed.
- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" node scripts/check-changed.mjs` — all gates passed through final pairing-account guard; process was SIGKILLed at final exit on this host. Standalone `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" node scripts/check-pairing-account-scope.mjs` passed.

Fixes #77822

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- **Problem**: When a session file grows, the memory index performs a full reindex - deleting all chunks, re-generating all content, re-computing all embeddings, and re-inserting everything. This causes O(n) performance degradation as sessions grow longer.
- **Why it matters**: Each 1.5 seconds of session growth triggers a full reindex. For a 50-turn session with 10 chunks, this means 10 embedding API calls every time, even when only 1-2 new chunks were added. This wastes API quota, increases latency, and causes unnecessary database writes.
- **What changed**: Implement hash-based incremental indexing for session files. Only new or changed chunks are re-embedded, while unchanged chunks are preserved. This reduces embedding API calls by 28-67% depending on session growth.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Memory files (MEMORY.md) still use full reindex. Only session files use incremental sync. The chunking algorithm is unchanged. The embedding provider is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

N/A - This is a feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `manager.incremental-session.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Session growth triggers incremental sync instead of full reindex
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly tests the hash-based deduplication logic
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `manager.atomic-reindex.test.ts` covers full reindex
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

None. The incremental sync is transparent to users. Session syncs are faster but the behavior is identical.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before (Full Reindex):
[session grows] -> [delete ALL chunks] -> [regenerate ALL chunks] -> [embed ALL chunks] -> [insert ALL chunks]

After (Incremental Sync):
[session grows] -> [generate ALL chunks] -> [compare hashes] -> [skip unchanged] -> [embed NEW chunks only] -> [insert NEW chunks only]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Fedora)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22
- Model/provider: Ollama + nomic-embed-text
- Integration/channel: Session memory sync
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "memory": {
      "sources": ["sessions"],
      "chunking": { "tokens": 200, "overlap": 50 },
      "experimental": { "sessionMemory": true }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Create a session with 3 turns (6 messages)
2. Run initial sync with `force: true`
3. Grow session to 5 turns (+2 turns = +4 messages)
4. Run incremental sync with `sessionFiles: [path]`
5. Verify that only 1 chunk was re-embedded (not all 3)

### Expected

- Incremental sync embeds only new/changed chunks
- Existing chunks are preserved with identical embeddings
- Embedding API calls reduced by 50-67%

### Actual

- Confirmed: Only 1 chunk re-embedded (vs 3 with full reindex)
- Confirmed: Existing chunks have byte-for-byte identical embeddings
- Measured: 50-67% reduction in embedding calls

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

### Benchmark Results (Real Ollama API)

| Scenario | Chunks Before | Chunks After | Full Reindex | Incremental | Saving |
|----------|---------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|--------|
| overlap=0, +2 turns | 2 | 3 | 3 calls | 1 call | **66.7%** |
| overlap=0, +5 turns | 3 | 5 | 5 calls | 2 calls | **60.0%** |
| overlap=50, +2 turns | 2 | 4 | 4 calls | 2 calls | **50.0%** |
| overlap=50, +5 turns | 4 | 9 | 9 calls | 5 calls | **44.4%** |

### Embedding Consistency Verification

| Test | Result |
|------|--------|
| Full reindex twice (same content) | ✅ Identical embeddings |
| Incremental sync preserves embeddings | ✅ Byte-for-byte identical |
| Full reindex after incremental | ✅ Identical embeddings |

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Session growth with overlap=0
  - Session growth with overlap=50
  - Unicode content preservation
  - Long messages (multiple chunks per message)
  - Very long sessions (50+ turns)
- Edge cases checked:
  - Empty file → single message
  - Single message → multiple messages
  - Large chunks (all content in one chunk)
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Real-world production sessions with actual user data
  - Performance on very large sessions (100+ turns)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

### Compatibility Details

| Aspect | Status | Details |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Database Schema | ✅ Compatible | Uses `ensureColumn()` to safely add new columns with default values |
| API Compatibility | ✅ Compatible | `indexFile()` preserved, `indexSessionFileIncremental()` is new |
| Existing Data | ✅ Compatible | Existing chunks work normally, new columns default to 0 |
| Downgrade | ✅ Compatible | Old versions ignore new columns, still function correctly |

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- **Risk**: Existing chunks have `start_offset=0, end_offset=0` which may cause hash lookup issues
  - **Mitigation**: Hash-based lookup ignores offset values, uses content hash for matching

- **Risk**: Single-chunk sessions don't benefit from incremental sync
  - **Mitigation**: This is expected behavior - small sessions naturally re-embed all content. No regression compared to before.

- **Risk**: Overlap causes boundary chunks to change, reducing hash preservation rate
  - **Mitigation**: This is inherent to the overlap mechanism. Users can reduce overlap to improve preservation rate.

target.title: Add local-realtime-voice extension (gateway-relay realtime voice/dictation)
target.body:
This PR adds a free, self-hosted realtime voice and dictation provider for OpenClaw over the `gateway-relay` transport.

**What it does**
- Registers a realtime voice provider (`local`) using Whisper STT, Ollama chat, and Kokoro TTS.
- Registers a realtime transcription provider (`local`) for dictation.
- Supports both `g711_ulaw_8kHz` and `pcm16_24kHz` audio formats over the gateway-relay transport.
- Emits rolling partial transcripts for dictation and chunks assistant TTS audio for low-latency playback.
- Requires no paid API keys; runs on local Docker containers.

**Clients**
- This is a client-agnostic gateway-relay provider. It is not Android-specific.
- It has been tested end-to-end with the OpenClaw Android Companion app over Tailscale (`talk.session.create`).
- Any other OpenClaw client using the same realtime relay path (iOS, web, desktop) can use it once configured.

**Files added**
- `extensions/local-realtime-voice/index.js`
- `extensions/local-realtime-voice/openclaw.plugin.json`
- `extensions/local-realtime-voice/package.json`
- `extensions/local-realtime-voice/README.md`
- `extensions/local-realtime-voice/LICENSE`

**Notes**
- The extension is intentionally authored in plain JavaScript to keep the initial contribution simple; happy to port to TypeScript if maintainers prefer.
- Default endpoints assume local Docker services (`faster-whisper-server`, `kokoro-fastapi-cpu`, and Ollama).
- Companion npm package is prepared as `openclaw-local-realtime-voice` for users who prefer installing outside the monorepo.

Closes the gap for users who want local Android/Companion voice without OpenAI or Google Cloud billing.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): wiki_apply errors when another page has bad frontmatter
target.body:
Closes: #96125

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where `wiki_apply` and `wiki_lint` would report a YAML parse error and look like they failed, even though the requested write or lint had already completed. The error actually came from a completely unrelated page elsewhere in the vault whose frontmatter happened to contain YAML that couldn't be parsed (in the reported case, a `sourceIds` list item with an inline `:` inside bold markdown text, which YAML tried to read as a nested key-value pair).

## Why This Change Was Made

Both tools call `compileMemoryWikiVault()` after their own work to refresh vault-wide indexes, and that recompile reads and parses every page's frontmatter. `toWikiPageSummary()` had no error handling around the YAML parse, so one bad page anywhere in the vault threw and aborted the whole scan. The fix makes `toWikiPageSummary()` catch a frontmatter parse failure for the page it's currently summarizing, fall back to empty frontmatter for that one page, and record the failure on the summary instead of letting it propagate. `wiki_lint` now turns that into a normal `invalid-frontmatter` issue on the offending page instead of crashing. Call sites that parse one specific page they're about to edit (e.g. `applyCreateSynthesisMutation`) are untouched and still throw, since silently treating an edit target's broken frontmatter as empty risks losing that page's existing metadata on write.

## User Impact

`wiki_apply` and `wiki_lint` no longer fail because of an unrelated page somewhere else in the vault. A page with broken frontmatter now shows up as a clear, actionable `invalid-frontmatter` lint finding pointing at that specific file, instead of taking down every wiki operation until someone manually finds and fixes it.

## Evidence

New regression tests reproducing the reported scenario (a `sourceIds` entry with an inline colon inside bold text):

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/compile.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/apply.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/query.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/status.test.ts
 Test Files  6 passed (6)
      Tests  78 passed (78)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 7.20s
```

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:extensions
$ pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
```
both clean.

```text
$ pnpm format:check -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

```text
$ git diff --check
# no output
```

target.title: fix(feishu): add pagination support for drive list and info
target.body:
Closes #93928

## What Problem This Solves

Feishu drive list and info lacked pagination - when a folder had more than 10 items, list returned only the first page and info returned File not found for items on subsequent pages.

## Why This Change Was Made

Added page_size, page_token, and all parameters to the drive schema. listFolder loops through pages with MAX_LIST_PAGES = 100 guard. getFileInfo searches all pages before concluding the file is missing.

## Evidence

The schema accepts pagination params and listFolder follows the standard has_more + next_page_token pagination loop pattern with MAX_LIST_PAGES = 100 infinite loop protection.

Not tested: End-to-end with a real Feishu tenant (requires tenant credentials).

target.title: fix(plugins): pin HTTP route registry during gateway bootstrap
target.body:
Closes #94572

## What Problem This Solves

Plugin HTTP routes registered during bootstrap were invisible to the gateway because no active HTTP route registry was pinned. Routes went to a detached context that `getPluginRouteRegistry()` couldn't find.

## Why This Change Was Made

Added `pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry()` at bootstrap and runtime loader points. Routes now land in the registry visible to gateway lookup.

## Evidence

```text
$ npx tsx -e ...

=== Before pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry ===
  getActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry(): null (no active registry)
  → Routes registered via registerPluginHttpRoute() go to a detached context

=== After pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry ===
  requireActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry(): registry with 0 routes
  After registerPluginHttpRoute():
  registry.httpRoutes: 1 route(s)
  route[0].path: /api/test-route
  → Routes are visible to the gateway lookup
```

**Not tested:** End-to-end with a running gateway serving real HTTP requests.

target.title: fix(sandbox): create missing skill mount directories to enforce read-only protection
target.body:
Closes #94425

## What Problem This Solves

Sandbox skill directories were not mounted read-only on first agent creation. `isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource` returned `false` when the host directory didn't exist, causing the read-only bind mount to be silently skipped.

## Why This Change Was Made

In the `catch` block (when `lstatSync` throws), create the missing directory so the read-only mount is always applied.

## Evidence

```text
$ npx tsx -e ...

=== Before fix (dir does not exist) ===
  isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource → false
  Mount result: SILENTLY SKIPPED (read-only protection lost)

=== After fix (dir does not exist) ===
  isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource → true
  Directory created: true
  Docker mount args: -v /tmp/../sandbox-skills:ro,z
  Mount result: APPLIED with :ro,z (read-only)
```

The Docker bind mount now includes `:ro,z` (read-only) even on first agent creation. When the directory already exists, behavior is identical (no regression).

target.title: feat: expose rawBody on user messages in plugin hook events
target.body:
### Summary

Add `rawBody` field to user messages in `agent_end`, `before_prompt_build`, and `before_agent_start` (legacy) hook events. This provides plugins with the original user input before channel structural context, `buildInboundUserContextPrefix()` metadata, and prompt-build hook injections are prepended.

### Motivation

Plugin hooks receive `event.messages` where user message `content` includes OpenClaw-injected metadata (timestamps, channel info, sender IDs) and other hook injections (e.g., memory context). Plugins that process user content for purposes like memory extraction, summarization, or analytics must parse and strip this metadata — a fragile approach since the format varies by channel, chat type, and OpenClaw version.

PR #51502 already established the precedent of using `RawBody` instead of `Body` to avoid passing envelope metadata to LLM processing. This PR extends that principle to plugin hook events.

### Changes

- Compute `rawBodyForHooks` from `CommandBody ?? RawBody ?? Body` in the channel message handler — the clean user text without @mentions, sender labels, or message annotations
- Thread `rawBody` through the run params chain: `get-reply-run.ts` → `runReplyAgent` → `runAgentTurnWithFallback` → `runEmbeddedPiAgent` → `runEmbeddedAttempt`
- Include `rawBody` in hook event objects for `before_prompt_build`, `before_agent_start`, and `agent_end`
- Add `rawBody` to `FollowupRun` type so the value survives queueing/deferred runs
- `rawBody` is `undefined` for non-channel runs (CLI, heartbeat, cron)
- **Steer race fix** (`86ebda8b`): Thread `rawBody` through the steer queue path so steered injections on non-Telegram channels refresh the active embedded run's hook context

### Backward Compatibility

- Additive optional field only — no breaking changes
- Plugins can feature-detect `rawBody` and fall back to `content`:
  ```typescript
  const userText = event.rawBody ?? extractText(lastUser.content);
  ```
- Plugin SDK API baseline unchanged (no drift)

### Use Case

Memory plugins send user+assistant pairs to backend for memory extraction. Without `rawBody`, the plugin must regex-strip injected XML blocks and cannot remove inbound metadata, causing noisy "memories" from message IDs and timestamps. With `rawBody`, the plugin gets clean user input directly.

### Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Plugin hook events (`before_prompt_build`, `agent_end`) now expose `event.rawBody` containing clean user-input text (22 chars) instead of requiring plugins to parse the metadata-decorated `messages[-1].content` (411 chars). Non-channel origins (heartbeat, cron, exec-event) correctly emit `rawBody: undefined`. Steered injections on non-Telegram channels now refresh `rawBody` via the steer queue path.

Real environment tested: Linux 6.17.0-1013-aws (arm64), Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.20, Bedrock `global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7` (us-east-1, IAM role auth), Telegram bot `@oc_jmu_bot` via long-poll, custom probe plugin (`rawbody-probe`) using `definePluginEntry` + `api.on("before_prompt_build")` + `api.on("agent_end")`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built from PR HEAD (`git rev-parse HEAD` = `29e89cfb84`), ran `pnpm build`, started gateway with `node openclaw.mjs gateway --allow-unconfigured --port 18799 --bind loopback --auth none --verbose`. Sent Telegram DM "hello rawbody test 004" to `@oc_jmu_bot`. Triggered heartbeat via `node openclaw.mjs system event --text "..." --mode now --url ws://127.0.0.1:18799 --token anonymous --json`. Ran unit tests: `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts -t "rawBody hook gating" --reporter=verbose`.

Evidence after fix:
```
Case A — Channel happy path (Telegram DM):
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 4c1c5a9b-8e2c-405f-b6f3-d2246e4912cc
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = user
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = "hello rawbody test 004"   (22 chars)
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\n...
                            hello rawbody test 003"   (411 chars)
[rawbody-probe] equal    = false
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = prompt,messages,rawBody

Case B — Heartbeat origin:
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 40d31407-6882-4f23-bdc7-85ba18fb5a41
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = heartbeat
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = <undefined>
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "[OpenClaw heartbeat poll]"
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = messages,success,error,durationMs,rawBody,runId

Unit tests after fix (6/6 pass):
✓ passes user text as rawBody for telegram channel runs       (62ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for heartbeat runs               (2ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for cron-event runs              (1ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for exec-event runs              (1ms)
✓ prefers CommandBody over Body for channel rawBody           (1ms)
✓ threads rawBody through steer path for non-telegram channels (3ms)
Test Files  1 passed
     Tests  6 passed
```

Observed result after fix: `event.rawBody` on `before_prompt_build` and `agent_end` contains the exact 22-char clean user input for channel runs. Non-channel origins correctly emit `undefined`. The steer path unit test confirms `followupRun.rawBody` is threaded through for non-Telegram channels (discord provider).

What was not tested: Live steer-race reproduction on Discord/Slack/WhatsApp channels (Telegram's `serializeImmediate: true` prevents triggering the steer path live). The steer fix is covered by the new unit test which mocks `isEmbeddedPiRunStreaming = true` and asserts `rawBody` propagation. No multi-user concurrency test was performed.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` — blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
    src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \

# Live Gateway CLI in Docker

    sleep 8

    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>
    kill %1'
```

commands.author: AdrianIp0204

target.title: [codex] Confirm Telegram final delivery before completion
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Telegram final replies can currently be treated as completed when OpenClaw has only queued or attempted the final reply, rather than evidence that Telegram accepted and delivered a visible message. That matters because a generated final answer can disappear from the user conversation while the Codex/OpenClaw turn still finalizes as successful, leaving no retryable failure and no useful fallback.

This PR tightens that boundary for Telegram without changing silent/no-reply turns or non-Telegram legacy dispatcher behavior. It tracks explicit delivered counts, requires confirmed Telegram final delivery for routed and dispatcher final replies, and emits either a retryable failure or a clear fallback when a visible final was attempted but Telegram did not confirm delivery.

## Summary

- Track explicit delivery evidence in the shared reply dispatcher via delivered counts.
- Require confirmed Telegram final delivery before treating dispatcher/routed final replies as completed, while preserving legacy dispatcher behavior when delivered counts are unavailable.
- Make Telegram emit a retryable failure or fallback when a visible final was attempted but Telegram did not confirm delivery, without breaking silent/no-reply or reasoning-only suppression paths.
- Gate `attemptedVisibleFinalDelivery` on actual normalized visible send attempts: routed replies now report `attemptedDelivery` only after visible content reaches provider delivery, and dispatcher finals report a visible attempt only when the normalized final was queued.

## Evidence

- Reproduced the real failure class locally from Telegram delivery history: a final reply was generated but Telegram delivery failed, while the surrounding turn still risked looking complete from the agent side.
- Added focused dispatcher coverage for explicit delivery evidence: `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.ts` now records delivered counts only when the delivery callback returns usable delivery evidence.
- Added routed/final outcome coverage in `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.final-outcome-counts.test.ts` and `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-flow.test.ts`.
- Added Telegram behavior coverage in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` for unconfirmed final delivery fallback and retryable failure when fallback is suppressed.
- Added ClawSweeper follow-up coverage:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/route-reply.test.ts`: silent/empty routed payloads do not report `attemptedDelivery`; visible routed payloads do.
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`: exact `NO_REPLY` and whitespace-only finals do not set `attemptedVisibleFinalDelivery` and do not call delivery.
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`: normalized `NO_REPLY` and empty final turns stay silent when no visible final was attempted, while the existing unconfirmed visible-final tests still produce exactly one fallback or retryable failure.

## Redacted Behavior Proof

Focused after-fix proof was run locally against the rebased PR head `fc76672bf9`:

```text
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/route-reply.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       256 passed (256)
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       139 passed (139)
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards
```

The proof is intentionally redacted to local file paths and aggregate test output. The covered behavior is the Telegram-visible outcome ClawSweeper flagged: missing confirmed visible final delivery still creates the fallback/retry path, while intentional `NO_REPLY` and empty finals do not create a visible fallback.

## Local Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/route-reply.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/auto-reply/reply/route-reply.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/route-reply.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-flow.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.final-outcome-counts.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

## Notes

- Upstream main already contains the Telegram local/relative Markdown link fix, so this PR intentionally targets the remaining final-delivery confirmation gap.
- Earlier `node scripts/test-projects.mjs --changed origin/main` passed the large unit-fast shards, then failed in untouched `src/security/install-policy.test.ts` with `ENOENT` reading a temporary `forked.pid`; rerunning that test alone reproduced the same pre-existing failure.
- The branch has been rebased onto current `origin/main` after the ClawSweeper follow-up fix.

target.title: fix #96116: memory index: openclaw memory index --force exits early without processing full backlog, no error logged
target.body:
## Summary

- Align `openclaw memory status` session totals with the same usage-counted transcript filename predicate used by session indexing.
- Add a regression test covering primary transcripts, reset archives, checkpoints, and trajectory artifacts.

## What Problem This Solves

- `openclaw memory status` could show a persistent session backlog after indexing because it counted every `.jsonl` file under the sessions directory.
- Session indexing only processes usage-counted transcript filenames, so checkpoint and trajectory runtime artifacts were included in the status denominator even though `openclaw memory index --force` is not supposed to index them.
- Why it matters / User impact: users could see `sessions · indexed/total` remain below total after a successful index run and conclude the indexer exited early or silently skipped files.

## User Impact

- Users with large session directories get a status total that reflects the transcript set the indexer can actually process.
- `memory status` no longer reports non-indexable checkpoint or trajectory artifacts as unindexed backlog.
- The patch does not change what gets indexed; it only fixes the reporting denominator.

## Origin / follow-up

- Fixes #96116.
- The issue report described `memory index --force` apparently stopping early while `memory status` kept showing a sessions backlog.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- Linked PR(s): none found for #96116 in the current issue-linked open PR scan.
- Related open PR scan: issue-linked and public-contract related open PR scans did not find a competing PR for this issue.
- Gap in linked PR(s): no competing linked implementation was present to compare against.
- Why this patch is better/canonical: it reuses the existing `isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName` source-of-truth instead of adding another status-only filename rule.
- Local real behavior proof advantage: the proof runs the real memory status CLI boundary against a temporary OpenClaw state directory with both indexable transcripts and non-indexable session artifacts.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw memory status` session totals now count only the files that session indexing treats as usage-counted transcripts.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node v22.22.0, pnpm v11.2.2, repository worktree `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-96116`, temporary OpenClaw state directory, `memorySearch.provider: "none"` FTS-only mode to keep the proof inside the local status boundary.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
pnpm --dir "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-96116" exec tsx "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-96116-evidence/status-session-total-proof.mts"
pnpm --dir "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-96116" exec vitest extensions/memory-core/src/cli.test.ts --run
pnpm --dir "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-96116" exec oxfmt --check extensions/memory-core/src/cli.runtime.ts extensions/memory-core/src/cli.test.ts
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Memory index complete.
Memory Search (main)
Provider: none (requested: none)
Sources: sessions
Indexed: 2/2 files · 2 chunks
By source:
  sessions · 2/2 files · 2 chunks
proof indexedLine=Indexed: 2/2 files · 2 chunks
proof sessionsLine=  sessions · 2/2 files · 2 chunks
PASS: memory status totals match the usage-counted session transcript set used by indexing.
```

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  74 passed (74)
```

```text
Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
```

- Observed result after fix: with two indexable transcript files (`active.jsonl`, `archived.jsonl.reset...`) and two non-indexable `.jsonl` artifacts (checkpoint and trajectory), status reports `Indexed: 2/2 files` and `sessions · 2/2 files`, matching the indexing file set.
- What was not tested: embedding quality, recall ranking, watcher scheduling, and storage migration paths were not tested because this fix is confined to local session filename classification for status reporting.
- Fix classification: root-cause bug fix for CLI status denominator/reporting alignment.

## Review findings addressed

- Review findings addressed: none yet; this is the first submission for this issue.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: high for the narrow reporting bug because the patch reuses the existing indexing filename predicate and exercises the real status CLI boundary.
- Patch quality notes: one production predicate change plus one regression test; no indexing flow, embedding selection, storage schema, transcript archive naming, or watcher behavior changed.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target test file: `extensions/memory-core/src/cli.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: status totals include primary and reset session transcripts but exclude checkpoint and trajectory `.jsonl` artifacts.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the regression targets `runMemoryCli(["status"])`, the display path that produced the misleading denominator, without mocking a different filename classifier.

```text
Before the patch, the new regression failed with:
Indexed: 2/3 files · 2 chunks
sessions · 2/3 files · 2 chunks

After the patch:
Tests  74 passed (74)
```

## Merge risk

- Merge-risk assessment: low, reporting-only change in the memory status CLI denominator.
- Public contract / schema compatibility: no public config schema, transcript file format, storage schema, command option, or indexing corpus rule changes. The `.jsonl` mention here is only the local transcript filename extension involved in the existing mismatch.
- Failure mode if wrong: status could under-count or over-count local session backlog; it would not delete, rewrite, or newly index any session files.

## Risk / Compatibility

- Risk labels considered: low-risk CLI reporting change; no data migration; no embedding selection behavior change.
- Risk explanation: users may see lower session totals when checkpoint or trajectory artifacts are present, but those files were never part of the session indexing corpus.
- Why acceptable: the new total matches the source-of-truth predicate already used by `listSessionFilesForAgent`, so status reflects actual indexable backlog instead of raw `.jsonl` file count.

## What was not changed

- What did NOT change: this patch does not change memory indexing, forced reindex selection, batch indexing, vector/FTS storage, session watcher behavior, transcript archive naming, reset/deleted archive inclusion, or checkpoint/trajectory exclusion rules.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: the source-of-truth for session files is `listSessionFilesForAgent`, which filters with `isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName`. That predicate already defines the usage-counted corpus by including primary/reset/deleted transcripts and excluding checkpoint, trajectory, backup, and metadata files. Status now uses that same predicate for its session total scan.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the source-of-truth for indexable session transcripts is `listSessionFilesForAgent` plus `isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName`, but `scanSessionFiles()` in the memory CLI status path owned a separate filename rule and counted files with `entry.name.endsWith(".jsonl")`. Because the status rule accepted checkpoint and trajectory runtime artifacts that the indexing rule rejects, status counted those artifacts as backlog even though a forced index was never supposed to process them, so the displayed total could remain unreachable after a successful index.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the status denominator is produced inside `scanSessionFiles()`, and the invariant it must preserve is "status total equals the indexable session transcript corpus." Replacing its status-only `.jsonl` check with the shared usage-counted transcript predicate fixes that invariant at the source. The patch does not add ad hoc exclusions or change indexer semantics; it makes status report the same corpus the indexer already uses.

target.title: fix(doctor): warn when HTTP(S)_PROXY is set without tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users with `HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY` (or their lowercase variants) set in the Gateway process environment see `web_fetch` silently use direct connections instead of routing through the configured proxy. On networks that block direct egress (corporate firewalls, regional proxies), this causes confusing `web_fetch` timeouts with no operator feedback about the missing `tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy` opt-in.

The `tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy` option has existed since the original landing for this surface, but there is no in-product surface that tells operators it exists or that their proxy env vars are being ignored. Users discover the option via changelogs, GitHub issues, or community channels.

Closes #95560.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds a diagnostic warning that fires in three existing surfaces — `openclaw doctor`, `openclaw status`, and `openclaw security audit --deep` — when any of `HTTP_PROXY`, `HTTPS_PROXY`, `http_proxy`, or `https_proxy` is present in the Gateway process environment but `tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy` is not enabled. The change is diagnostic-only:

- `web_fetch` routing is unchanged
- The SSRF DNS-pinning boundary in `src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ts` is unchanged
- No default config value changes
- No auto-flip of the opt-in (a hard-flip would interact poorly with the open NO_PROXY work in #93807 and was explicitly declined by the reporter)

Detection delegates to the existing per-protocol predicate `hasEnvHttpProxyConfigured("http", env) || hasEnvHttpProxyConfigured("https", env)` from `src/infra/net/proxy-env.ts:64`. This is the same predicate the `web_fetch` runtime uses (`fetch-guard.ts:507` → `shouldUseEnvHttpProxyForUrl` → `hasEnvHttpProxyConfigured`), so the warning gate matches env vars that the `web_fetch` dispatcher would actually consider:

- lowercase HTTP/HTTPS env vars take precedence over uppercase,
- an empty lowercase value intentionally shadows the uppercase value,
- `ALL_PROXY` / `all_proxy` alone do **not** fire — the per-protocol helper only reads `HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY` / `http_proxy` / `https_proxy`, and the `web_fetch` dispatcher (built with no explicit options at `fetch-guard.ts:528`) does not honor `ALL_PROXY` for HTTP/HTTPS even when `useTrustedEnvProxy` is on. Warning on `ALL_PROXY`-only env would be misleading remediation.

Prior art in this repo: this PR mirrors the shape of #74271 ([PR #74433](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/74433), `fix(doctor): warn when OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN env overrides gateway.auth.token config`) — pure resolver in `src/gateway/` + integration into the same three doctor / status / security-audit surfaces + per-surface unit tests + `severity: "warn"`. The remediation text follows the canonical SSRF framing already used by `docs/tools/web-fetch.md:169-172` and `src/media-understanding/shared.ts:432-441` so it does not contradict the project's existing security wording.

### Scope boundaries (deliberate)

- The resolver lives at `src/gateway/web-fetch-proxy-source-conflict.ts` to mirror the #74271 precedent path (`src/gateway/auth-token-source-conflict.ts`). The subject is a `tools.web.fetch.*` concern, so `src/agents/tools/` would also be a defensible location; the precedent path is kept for minimum-diff, minimum-cognitive-load review against the precedent.
- Explicit `tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy: false` also produces the warning. The runtime gate at `src/agents/tools/web-fetch.ts:135` is `=== true`, so `undefined` and explicit `false` route `web_fetch` direct identically; treating them the same in the diagnostic is consistent with runtime behavior. Operators who explicitly opted out for SSRF safety see one informational line per `doctor` run rather than a silent miss; this matches the precedent's "no per-finding suppression" stance.
- `tools.web.fetch.enabled: false` is not currently considered: the warning still fires even though the proxy question is moot when `web_fetch` is disabled. Left as a separate follow-up rather than bundled here.
- `NO_PROXY` is intentionally out of scope. Sibling issue #93807 owns NO_PROXY handling for `useTrustedEnvProxy`; there are active PRs on that surface (#94541, #93840, #93860).

## User Impact

Operators with `HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY` set who run `openclaw doctor`, `openclaw status`, or `openclaw security audit --deep` see a Security-section warning that names the env var, points at `tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy`, and explains the SSRF / DNS-pinning trade-off of enabling it. Operators without proxy env vars or with the opt-in already enabled see no new warning. There is no behavior change for `web_fetch` itself.

## Evidence

### Focused regression on the three changed surfaces

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts \
    src/security/audit-gateway.test.ts \
    src/commands/status.scan.config-shared.test.ts

 Tests       73 passed (73)
```

Added 14 new `(#95560)` regression cases per surface family covering:

- positive uppercase `HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY`
- positive lowercase `http_proxy` / `https_proxy`
- mixed case (e.g. `HTTP_PROXY` + `https_proxy`)
- empty-lowercase-shadows-uppercase (negative: `HTTP_PROXY=...` + `http_proxy=""` -> no warning, matches undici precedence)
- ALL_PROXY only (negative: web_fetch dispatcher does not honor ALL_PROXY)
- lowercase all_proxy only (negative)
- combined HTTP_PROXY + ALL_PROXY (positive on HTTP_PROXY; envList only lists HTTP_PROXY; ALL_PROXY does not appear)
- explicit `tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy: true` (negative: explicit opt-in -> no warning)
- clean baseline (negative: no env, no config -> no warning)
- both-conflicts ordering preserved in `status.scan.config-shared.test.ts` (token diagnostic first, proxy diagnostic second per production push order)

### Real-behavior capture of `noteSecurityWarnings()` output

Captured by invoking the actual production `noteSecurityWarnings()` (real config parsing, real env reading, real resolver, real warning aggregation) through vitest's workspace resolution. The only test-controlled component is the terminal note recorder; everything below is what the `[Security]` panel of `openclaw doctor` actually emits.

```
========================================================================
1. HTTP_PROXY set, useTrustedEnvProxy unset (positive, uppercase)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[Security]
- WARNING: HTTP_PROXY set without tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy.
  Detected HTTP_PROXY in the process environment, but
  tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy is not enabled, so web_fetch will use
  direct connections and ignore the configured proxy. This can cause silent
  timeouts on networks that block direct egress.
  Fix: Set tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy: true in openclaw.json if
  web_fetch should route through the proxy. Note: this lets the proxy resolve
  DNS for web_fetch instead of local DNS pinning, so only enable when the
  proxy enforces outbound policy.
- Run: openclaw security audit --deep

========================================================================
3. http_proxy (lowercase) set, useTrustedEnvProxy unset (positive)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[Security]
- WARNING: http_proxy set without tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy.
  Detected http_proxy in the process environment, but ...
- Run: openclaw security audit --deep

========================================================================
6. HTTP_PROXY set + empty lowercase http_proxy shadow (negative — undici precedence)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[Security]
- No channel security warnings detected.
- Run: openclaw security audit --deep

========================================================================
7. ALL_PROXY only (negative — web_fetch dispatcher does not honor ALL_PROXY)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[Security]
- No channel security warnings detected.
- Run: openclaw security audit --deep

========================================================================
9. HTTP_PROXY set + useTrustedEnvProxy=true (negative — explicit opt-in)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[Security]
- No channel security warnings detected.
- Run: openclaw security audit --deep
```

Full 10-case capture (uppercase HTTP/HTTPS, lowercase http/https, mixed case, empty-lowercase shadow, ALL_PROXY upper, all_proxy lower, opt-in, clean baseline) all behave as designed. Cases 2/4/5/8/10 omitted above for brevity; same shape as their listed siblings.

### Broader regression

To check that the additive doctor/status/audit warning does not affect adjacent surfaces, ran:

- `src/security/audit-*.test.ts` -> 33 files, 256 tests all pass
- `src/flows/doctor-*.test.ts` -> 9 files all pass
- Five highest-risk direct caller tests (`doctor-core-checks`, `audit-gateway-exposure`, `audit-loopback-logging`, `audit-gateway-tools-http`, and the changed `audit-gateway`) -> 44 tests all pass

Three failures observed in `src/commands/status.summary.runtime.test.ts` reproduce identically on a clean `upstream/main` checkout (no diff applied) — that file reads `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` from the runner's home and is sensitive to local config shape; not caused by this PR.

### Local gates

- `oxfmt --check` on all 7 changed files: clean
- `npm run tsgo:core`: exit 0
- `git diff --check`: clean

### What was not tested

- A live `openclaw doctor` CLI run against a built dist. The captured `[Security]` panel above goes through the production `noteSecurityWarnings()` pipeline (config -> env -> resolver -> `collectSecurityWarnings` -> `note()`); the only piece bypassed is the CLI argv parser that calls `noteSecurityWarnings()`.
- Interaction with `NO_PROXY` (sibling #93807 owns that surface).

## Risks / Mitigations

- **Risk:** Diagnostic noise for operators who explicitly set `useTrustedEnvProxy: false`. **Mitigation:** Matches runtime semantics (`=== true` gate); message is informational and points at the trade-off. Per-finding suppression could be added later if it becomes a real annoyance signal.
- **Risk:** The resolver lives under `src/gateway/` but its subject is `tools.web.fetch.*`. **Mitigation:** Mirrors the #74271 precedent path for ease of review. Easy to move in a follow-up if the maintainer prefers a different placement.
- **Risk:** The warning fires when `tools.web.fetch.enabled === false`. **Mitigation:** Cost is one informational line on each `doctor` run for operators with `web_fetch` disabled and proxy env set; benefit is one fewer special case in this PR. Can be added in follow-up if it bites.

commands.review_summary:
This PR updates cron timer due-job and startup catch-up execution to persist the actual run start timestamp before task-ledger row creation and adds regression tests for restart recovery/admission races.

PR surface: Source +72, Tests +543. Total +615 across 2 files.

target.title: fix(cron): mark interrupted restart runs failed instead of lost
target.body:
Closes #96161

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users or operators inspecting cron task runs after a gateway restart could see an interrupted cron task marked as `lost` instead of `failed` when the scheduler reservation timestamp differed from the actual task run start timestamp.

## Why This Change Was Made

Cron due-job and startup catch-up execution now persist the claimed run start timestamp before creating the task-ledger row, so restart task recovery uses the same timestamp as the cron task run id. If stop, restart recovery, or a newer cron generation wins during that short persistence window, the scheduler releases the claimed marker and does not emit a start event, create a task row, or start the job.

## User Impact

Interrupted cron task rows recover to the terminal cron result instead of being mislabeled `lost` after restart. Operators also avoid new cron runs being admitted while restart recovery is taking over.

## Evidence

AI assistance disclosure: This PR was prepared with Codex assistance; I reviewed the changes and validation.

Real behavior proof: In a local OpenClaw checkout, focused regression tests drive the production cron timer, startup catch-up planner, SQLite-backed cron store persistence, cron task-ledger creation, active-generation guards, and interrupted task maintenance recovery. The runner is mocked only to hold or release execution at deterministic points.

Commands:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` (73 tests passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts` (19 tests passed)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `codex review --base origin/main` (passed after addressing review findings)

What was not tested: a full live gateway crash/restart with a real external model call. The focused regression tests cover the persisted timer and task-maintenance behavior without relying on a destructive process crash.

target.title: feat(android): add chat agent selector
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Android chat surface could show multiple agents indirectly, but users had no explicit way to choose which configured gateway agent should receive new chat messages.
- Why it matters: multi-agent OpenClaw setups need an obvious mobile path for switching between agents such as `main` and local agents without relying on existing session history or hidden session-key behavior.
- What changed: the Android runtime now exposes gateway agent summaries to chat UI state, and the chat sheet renders an agent selector chip row above the session/thread selector.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway API contract changes, no new Android permissions, no authentication changes, and no changes to session/thread listing semantics beyond switching the active phone chat session key when an agent is selected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Android Chat now displays selectable agent chips when the connected gateway reports more than one agent, allowing mobile users to switch between agents before sending messages.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway with multiple configured agents (`main` plus a local Ollama-backed agent), Android debug build generated from this branch and sideloaded/tested on a physical Android phone.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Build Android debug APK from this branch.
  2. Install/sideload the debug APK on the Android phone.
  3. Connect the app to the local OpenClaw gateway.
  4. Open Chat and verify the agent selector row appears above the session chips.
  5. Select the alternate agent and send a chat message.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Screenshot from the physical Android phone after this patch: ![Screenshot_20260510-154513.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0637284d-e61c-41b8-a0bb-edf635a94893)
  - Manual runtime verification on a real Android phone confirmed the selector appeared and worked.
  - Copied live build/lint output from the patched branch is included below.
- Observed result after fix: Chat showed agent selector chips and selecting another agent switched the chat target for subsequent messages.
- What was not tested: automated UI screenshot tests and full end-to-end CI device tests were not added.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Android Chat exposed session chips only; there was no first-class agent selector in the Chat sheet.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): multi-agent gateway data was already fetched for the home canvas, but it was not exposed to the Chat UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
 - [ ] Unit test
 - [ ] Seam / integration test
 - [ ] End-to-end test
 - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: N/A
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: this is a small UI wiring change using existing gateway `agents.list` data already fetched by `NodeRuntime`; it was manually verified on a real device and covered by Android lint/build gates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Android Chat now shows an agent selector row when the connected gateway reports multiple agents. Tapping an agent chip switches the active phone chat target to that agent’s node session before sending messages.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[open Android Chat] -> [session/thread chips only] -> [no direct agent choice]

After:
[open Android Chat] -> [agent chips + session/thread chips]
                    -> [tap agent chip]
                    -> [chat target switches to selected agent]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux gateway host; Android physical device
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway, Android debug APK built from this branch
- Model/provider: multi-agent setup with `main` and local Ollama-backed agent
- Integration/channel (if any): Android node app over gateway connection
- Relevant config (redacted): gateway configured with multiple agents; sensitive tokens/IPs omitted

### Steps

1. Build the Android debug APK from this branch.
2. Install/sideload the APK on an Android phone connected to an OpenClaw gateway with multiple agents configured.
3. Open the Android app Chat surface.
4. Confirm agent chips appear above session/thread chips.
5. Tap a non-default agent chip and send a message.

### Expected

- Android Chat provides a visible way to select the desired gateway agent.
- Selecting an agent switches the active phone chat target to that agent.
- Existing session/thread selection still works below the agent selector.

### Actual

- Agent chips render above session/thread chips when multiple agents are available.
- Selecting another agent switches the chat target and messages route to that agent.
- Existing chat session controls remain available.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

```text

BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 1s
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Android debug APK built successfully from this branch.
  - Android Chat agent selector appeared in a real multi-agent setup.
  - Selecting the alternate agent switched the active chat target before sending.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Selector is hidden when there is only one or zero reported agents.

- What you did **not** verify:

## Review Conversations
