target.title: fix(scripts): warn when windows-node tag is behind latest stable release
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a lightweight check in the release-candidate checklist script that warns when the specified `--windows-node-tag` is behind the latest stable release of `openclaw-windows-node`.

**Problem**

Issue #90953: an Italian Windows user could not install OpenClaw because `wsl.exe --version` output was localized (`Versione WSL:`) and the `openclaw-windows-node` v0.6.0 parser could not handle it. The fix existed in v0.6.3 but nothing alerted maintainers at release-cutting time.

**Solution**

Add two small helpers in `scripts/release-candidate-checklist.mjs`:

- `fetchLatestWindowsNodeTag()` — queries the GitHub API for the latest stable windows-node release
- `compareWindowsNodeTags(tag1, tag2)` — semver comparison for `vX.Y.Z` tags

When the stable-release workflow runs with `--windows-node-tag <tag>`, it now warns on stderr if the chosen tag is outdated:

```
[WARN] Windows Node tag v0.6.0 is behind latest v0.6.3. Consider using --windows-node-tag v0.6.3 for the latest fixes.
```

The warning is non-blocking — the release can still proceed — but it surfaces the drift before publishing.

**What changed**

- `scripts/release-candidate-checklist.mjs` — +43 lines (two new exported functions + warning in `main()`)
- `test/scripts/release-candidate-checklist.test.ts` — +73 lines (8 new test cases)

**Out of scope**

- No new scripts, workflows, GitHub Actions permissions, or PowerShell
- Does not change the parser in `openclaw-windows-node` (that fix is already in v0.6.3)
- Does not retroactively fix past releases

## Tests

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/release-candidate-checklist.test.ts
# Test Files  1 passed (1)
# Tests       39 passed (39)
```

## Live proof

- `fetchLatestWindowsNodeTag()` against live API → `v0.6.3`
- `compareWindowsNodeTags("v0.6.0", "v0.6.3")` → `-3` (detects the drift from #90953)
- `compareWindowsNodeTags("v0.6.3", "v0.6.0")` → `3` (ahead)

Closes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/90953

target.title: Add marketplace feed entries command
target.body:
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:start -->
## Full hosted marketplace feed stack

We are pausing this stack at PR8 for Vincent's review and takeover/shaping before wiring hosted marketplace data into startup, install, or automatic refresh behavior.

- PR1 [#95846](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95846): refactors the bundled external plugin catalog toward the feed-shaped data model.
- PR2 [#95868](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95868): adds the lazy hosted marketplace feed loader for the configured public feed URL, with guarded HTTPS fetch, checksum, schema, size, timeout, and bundled fallback behavior.
- PR3 [#95877](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95877): adds accepted hosted snapshot fallback so a failed or unchanged hosted fetch can reuse the last valid feed payload.
- PR4 [#95964](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95964): persists hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw state.
- PR5 [#95969](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95969): adds marketplace source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.
- PR6 [#95981](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95981): adds configurable `marketplaces.feeds` and `marketplaces.sources` profiles.
- PR7 [#96155](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96155): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` as the explicit manual snapshot refresh path.
- PR8 [#96158](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96158): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` as the explicit manual read path over hosted, snapshot, or bundled fallback entries.

Next after review, not in this stack yet: decide whether PR9 should wire marketplace entries into install/search/startup, add automatic conditional refresh using `ETag` / `Last-Modified`, or move to ClawHub producer-side validation for `/v1/feeds/plugins` and `/v1/feeds/skills`.
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:end -->

## Summary

This is PR8 in the hosted OpenClaw marketplace feed stack. It adds the first explicit read path over the configured hosted marketplace feed: `openclaw plugins marketplace entries`.

The command lists normalized entries from the configured/default OpenClaw marketplace feed and reports whether the data came from a fresh hosted payload, an accepted hosted snapshot, or bundled fallback data. It also supports `--offline` so maintainers can prove the refreshed snapshot path without fetching the live feed.

## Stack context

- RFC: openclaw/rfcs#19 defines the hosted marketplace feed direction.
- PR1: shape the bundled external plugin catalog as feed-like data.
- PR2: lazy hosted feed fetch from the configured public marketplace feed.
- PR3: hosted snapshot fallback.
- PR4: persistent snapshot storage in OpenClaw state.
- PR5: source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.
- PR6: configurable marketplace feed/source profiles.
- PR7: `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` manual snapshot refresh.
- PR8: this PR, `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` manual feed/snapshot listing.

## What changed

- Adds `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` with `--feed-profile`, `--feed-url`, `--offline`, and `--json`.
- Adds an explicit `offline` mode to the hosted catalog loader so callers can read the latest accepted snapshot without doing a network request.
- Normalizes listed entries into id, label, kind, package/version, and install metadata.
- Updates the plugin CLI docs to include `marketplace entries` and `marketplace refresh`.
- Adds focused CLI and hosted catalog tests for parser dispatch, JSON output, bundled fallback output, and offline snapshot reuse.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt docs/cli/plugins.md src/cli/plugins-cli.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.runtime.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.lazy.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-refresh.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-entries.test.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/cli/plugins-cli.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.runtime.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.lazy.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-refresh.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-entries.test.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `CI=1 timeout 240s node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/plugins-cli.lazy.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-refresh.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-entries.test.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
- `timeout 300s pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `codex review -c model=gpt-5.5 --base origin/feeds-hosted-catalog-marketplace-refresh`

## Review note

The final Codex review reported no discrete correctness issues. Its internal sandbox could not run tests because the read-only review sandbox could not create the heavy-check lock directory, but the local targeted tests above passed.

target.title: Add hosted external catalog feed loader
target.body:
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:start -->
## Full hosted marketplace feed stack

We are pausing this stack at PR8 for Vincent's review and takeover/shaping before wiring hosted marketplace data into startup, install, or automatic refresh behavior.

- PR1 [#95846](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95846): refactors the bundled external plugin catalog toward the feed-shaped data model.
- PR2 [#95868](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95868): adds the lazy hosted marketplace feed loader for the configured public feed URL, with guarded HTTPS fetch, checksum, schema, size, timeout, and bundled fallback behavior.
- PR3 [#95877](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95877): adds accepted hosted snapshot fallback so a failed or unchanged hosted fetch can reuse the last valid feed payload.
- PR4 [#95964](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95964): persists hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw state.
- PR5 [#95969](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95969): adds marketplace source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.
- PR6 [#95981](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95981): adds configurable `marketplaces.feeds` and `marketplaces.sources` profiles.
- PR7 [#96155](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96155): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` as the explicit manual snapshot refresh path.
- PR8 [#96158](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96158): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` as the explicit manual read path over hosted, snapshot, or bundled fallback entries.

Next after review, not in this stack yet: decide whether PR9 should wire marketplace entries into install/search/startup, add automatic conditional refresh using `ETag` / `Last-Modified`, or move to ClawHub producer-side validation for `/v1/feeds/plugins` and `/v1/feeds/skills`.
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:end -->

## Journey Context

This is step 2 of the hosted OpenClaw feed path from RFC 19. The stack is [#95846](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95846) -> [#95868](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95868) -> [#95877](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95877). #95846 made the bundled catalog feed-shaped. This PR adds the guarded hosted fetch path for that same marketplace feed, while leaving the existing synchronous bundled-catalog startup path unchanged.

## What This PR Does

- Adds an async hosted loader for `https://register.openclaw.ai/official-marketplace.json`.
- Routes hosted fetches through the existing SSRF guard with HTTPS required and the register host allowlisted.
- Sends optional `If-None-Match` and `If-Modified-Since` headers so callers can perform freshness checks.
- Reads response bodies with byte and chunk-timeout limits.
- Keeps the network guard import lazy so bundled-catalog cold paths do not load the guarded network stack.
- Computes `sha256` metadata for the hosted response body.
- Validates the hosted document against the feed-shaped official external plugin catalog schema.
- Falls back to the bundled catalog on network, status, checksum, size, JSON, or schema failures.

## What This PR Intentionally Does Not Do

This PR does not wire the hosted loader into startup, add persistent snapshots, add source-profile selection, add auth, add signed envelopes, add regional feeds, or change plugin install/search behavior. #95877 adds the loader-level last-known-good snapshot contract on top of this fetch path.

## How To Review

Review this as the hosted transport and validation layer. The important contract is: hosted marketplace feed can be tried safely, but any unsafe or unusable hosted result falls back to the bundled catalog instead of changing current behavior.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `CI=1 timeout 180s node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` (22 tests)
- `timeout 300s pnpm tsgo:core:test`

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve abort after dispatch rejection
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Preserves the terminal `chat.abort` outcome when `chat.send` dispatch rejects after abort already completed.
- Preserves the real dispatch `error` outcome when abort arrives only during awaited error persistence, instead of letting a late abort mask/cache the error.
- Keeps current `main` dispatch-error lifecycle behavior intact: pre-agent dispatch errors still persist failed session state and emit `sessions.changed`.
- Adds regressions for both race orderings and prevents stale abort tombstones from leaking into later same-id retries.

Why does this matter now?

- `chat.send` caches terminal abort results for abort races. The post-dispatch rejection path still had two bad windows: abort-first could be overwritten by a late dispatch error, and error-first could expose `aborted` while the handler was awaiting fallback persistence.
- The conflict refresh also picked up the current `main` dispatch-error lifecycle path, so the merge has to preserve both the abort/error terminal-state contract and the session lifecycle contract.

What is the intended outcome?

- Same-run retry after an abort-first race observes the terminal timeout/aborted payload, not a synthetic dispatch error.
- Same-run retry after an error-first race observes the dispatch error while the error dedupe is live; a later retry can start normally after error dedupe cleanup.
- Late abort in the error-first path returns `aborted:false`, emits no aborted chat state, and does not recreate the abort tombstone.
- Pre-agent dispatch error continues to persist failed session lifecycle state and emit `sessions.changed` with `reason:"chat.dispatch-error"`.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Client-side terminal ACK handling.
- The original pre-dispatch attachment-preparation abort race, which is already fixed on `main`.

What does success look like?

- The PR diff stays limited to `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts` and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`.
- Focused Gateway regression, sibling lifecycle suite, typecheck, lint, diff hygiene, production-handler proof, and real Gateway/WebSocket RPC proof all pass on the refreshed head.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The `.catch` path snapshots whether abort had already won before any awaited fallback persistence.
- The error-winning path closes abortability, clears late abort state, and writes same-run error dedupe before awaiting fallback persistence.
- The session-lifecycle error event is still persisted/emitted after active-run cleanup.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Fixes #90999

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #84176 and #84306.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Post-dispatch `chat.send` rejection could produce contradictory terminal state around `chat.abort`: abort-first could be overwritten by dispatch error, and error-first could briefly expose/cache `aborted` before final dispatch `error`. The conflict refresh also had to preserve current `main` dispatch-error lifecycle persistence.

- Real environment tested:

Linux source checkout at local conflict-repair head `0388ac3830a6215c738f518922d202ddbef23ff3`, Node/Vitest through repo scripts. The merge commit has parents `e16d6f5fcfeabe193b49883fe89061dead8efad1` and `9ff7abc8989c1770856b27d5b06e6bb6695ce666`. A pre-publish fetch advanced `upstream/main` to `258b83c4386cdaf2cee0d64af64d20bb09443347`; merge-tree stayed clean, no same-surface files changed on `main` since the merge base, and the PR-style diff stayed exactly the two Gateway files.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```shell
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-pr91000-0388-rerun.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-prod-pr91000-0388-rerun.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD -- src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
git merge-tree --write-tree upstream/main HEAD
OPENCLAW_PROBE_ROOT=/tmp/openclaw-pr91000-conflict3-20260624T000548+0800.1817729 node --experimental-test-module-mocks --import tsx /tmp/pr91000-behavior-probe-dynamic.mts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/pr91000-websocket-proof-dynamic.test.ts
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal/proof captures from this branch:

```shell
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
Test Files 2 passed (2)
Tests 86 passed (86)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts
Test Files 2 passed (2)
Tests 72 passed (72)

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-pr91000-0388-rerun.tsbuildinfo
passed

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-prod-pr91000-0388-rerun.tsbuildinfo
passed

$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

$ git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD -- src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
no output

$ git merge-tree --write-tree upstream/main HEAD
clean
```

Production-handler proof on head `0388ac3830a6215c738f518922d202ddbef23ff3`:

```text
abort-first dispatch rejection: chat.abort returned aborted=true, dispatch saw AbortSignal.aborted=true, terminalDedupe.status=timeout, summary=aborted, chatBroadcastStates=[aborted], errorBroadcastCount=0
error-first late abort: chat.abort returned aborted=false, retryDuringPending.status=error with UNAVAILABLE, terminalDedupe.status=error, lateAbortTombstonePresentAfterError=false, midWindowChatBroadcastStates=[], chatBroadcastStates=[error], later retry starts
```

Real Gateway + WebSocket RPC proof on head `0388ac3830a6215c738f518922d202ddbef23ff3`:

```text
Test Files 3 passed (3)
Tests 6 passed (6)
abort-first over WebSocket: chat.send ACK status=started; chat.abort response aborted=true; dispatch saw AbortSignal.aborted=true; chat broadcast states=[aborted]
error-first late abort over WebSocket: chat.send ACK status=started; late chat.abort response aborted=false; same-id retry during pending fallback returned cached status=error with UNAVAILABLE; mid-window chat broadcast states=[]; final chat broadcast states=[error]
```

- Observed result after fix:

Abort-first dispatch rejection preserves the terminal timeout/aborted result and emits only the aborted chat state. Error-first dispatch rejection makes the error terminal before awaited fallback persistence; late abort returns `aborted:false`, same-id retry during the pending fallback receives the cached error, no aborted chat state is emitted, and retry after deleting the error dedupe can start normally. The added lifecycle assertion confirms `sessions.changed` still reports `reason:"chat.dispatch-error"`, `status:"failed"`, and `hasActiveRun:false`.

- What was not tested:

No browser UI, native client, or live provider/model E2E run was performed. Transport proof uses a real Gateway server plus real WebSocket RPC client, controlling only the dispatch boundary through the repo test seam.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints:

The race is deterministic once dispatch rejection, fallback persistence, and abort timing are controlled. Both probes control that boundary, avoiding flaky live-provider timing.

- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

Earlier diagnostics on `main` reproduced both bad windows: abort-first ended with `[aborted,error]`; error-first late abort returned `aborted:true`, cached timeout/aborted during pending fallback, left a tombstone, and emitted `[aborted,error]`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-pr91000-0388-rerun.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-prod-pr91000-0388-rerun.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD -- src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree upstream/main HEAD`
- `OPENCLAW_PROBE_ROOT=/tmp/openclaw-pr91000-conflict3-20260624T000548+0800.1817729 node --experimental-test-module-mocks --import tsx /tmp/pr91000-behavior-probe-dynamic.mts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/pr91000-websocket-proof-dynamic.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added/updated coverage for abort-before-dispatch-reject preserving terminal abort state.
- Added/updated coverage for dispatch-reject-before-late-abort preserving terminal error state, rejecting late abort, serving cached error to same-id retry while fallback persistence is pending, and allowing a later retry after error dedupe cleanup.
- Added coverage that the current dispatch-error session lifecycle behavior still emits failed/no-active-run `sessions.changed` after the conflict refresh.
- Refreshed the branch against current `main`; the latest pre-publish fetch had no same-surface drift and clean merge-tree.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The original abort-first regression failed because `chat:<runId>` cached `status:"error"` instead of the expected timeout/aborted payload.
- The error-first probe found `chat.abort` could still return `aborted:true` during pending fallback persistence, and broadcasts could include both `aborted` and `error` for the same run.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Terminal abort/error ordering is now stable for post-dispatch rejection races.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Abort/error ordering in the `chat.send` async cleanup path, especially while fallback transcript persistence is pending, plus preserving dispatch-error session lifecycle persistence after active-run cleanup.

How is that risk mitigated?

Both race orderings are covered, the sibling session-lifecycle suite still passes, and the added lifecycle assertion checks the failed session notification emitted after active-run cleanup. The WebSocket proof exercises the real Gateway server/RPC transport with deterministic race timing.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the refreshed conflict-repair head and the updated real Gateway/WebSocket RPC proof.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

After branch/body refresh, CI and maintainer/bot review are pending.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

No new bot comment was addressed. This updates exact base/head/proof evidence after conflict repair while preserving proof scope.

target.title: fix(agents): bound runtime tool list projection
target.body:
## Summary

- Reject invalid or excessive runtime tool-list lengths before schema projection iterates plugin-controlled array-likes.
- Preserve the existing per-entry diagnostics path for normal lists, while reporting malformed list metadata as a bounded `tool[0]` diagnostic.
- Add a regression for a proxy that lies about `length` and previously caused `filterRuntimeCompatibleTools()` to silently drop the whole tool set with no diagnostic.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: hostile runtime tool-list metadata can no longer make schema projection silently treat the active tool surface as empty, or turn projection into an unbounded loop.

Real environment tested: local linked OpenClaw worktree on current `origin/main`; broad remote gates attempted but blocked by missing operator auth.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next182-red nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts --testNamePattern="reports invalid runtime tool list lengths" --reporter=verbose
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next182-rebased nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts --reporter=verbose
nodenv exec node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/tool-schema-projection.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
blacksmith testbox list --all
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --label next182-check-changed --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"
```

Evidence after fix: the targeted red test failed pre-fix with `expected { tools: [], diagnostics: [] } to deeply equal ...`; after the patch, `src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts` passed 11/11, oxlint exited cleanly, `git diff --check` exited cleanly, and branch autoreview was clean with `overall: patch is correct (0.83)`.

Observed result after fix: malformed `length` now produces a diagnostic with `runtime tool list length is invalid` instead of dropping all tools silently; normal compatible tools still pass the existing projection tests.

What was not tested: `pnpm check:changed` could not run remotely because `blacksmith testbox list --all` reported `not authenticated`, and direct AWS Crabbox failed with coordinator 401 on run and lease creation.

target.body:
## Summary
- guard plugin tool descriptor reads so malformed `name`, `execute`, `description`, or `parameters` access cannot abort sibling tool resolution
- pin each accepted plugin tool to one stable name before allow/deny policy, manifest filtering, metadata, descriptor cache, and cached execution
- keep descriptor caching on validated required fields while treating optional metadata getters (`label`, `displaySummary`) as best-effort

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts` - passed 2 files / 81 tests on `4c0d9558086`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/plugins/tools.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/plugins/tools.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` - clean after review-driven fixes, `overall: patch is correct (0.82)`

Blocked broader proof:
- `blacksmith testbox list --all` failed: not authenticated; requires `blacksmith auth login`
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --label next181-check-changed-refresh --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` failed before lease creation: coordinator `POST /v1/runs` and `POST /v1/leases` returned HTTP 401 unauthorized
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --label next181-check-changed-refresh --shell -- "env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed"` failed before remote execution: Blacksmith not authenticated

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: malformed plugin tool descriptor access no longer crashes `resolvePluginTools()` or bypasses tool policy before healthy sibling tools resolve.

Real environment tested: linked OpenClaw worktree on current `origin/main` `18ecb82034d`, using repo Vitest and lint/format wrappers.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`; focused `oxfmt`, `oxlint`, and `git diff --check` commands listed above; local autoreview on the final patch.

Evidence after fix: focused plugin resolver/cache tests passed 81 tests. Regressions cover unreadable parameters, parameters that become unreadable before descriptor caching, unreadable descriptions, unreadable/changing/unstable names, descriptor traps, non-configurable stable and unstable names, and optional metadata getters during cache capture. Autoreview initially found two real issues around required `description` and required `parameters`; both were fixed and the final review was clean.

Observed result after fix: bad plugin tools are diagnosed and skipped while valid sibling tools remain available. Accepted tools use one stable name through policy, metadata, descriptor caching, and cached execution. Descriptor cache entries are built from already-validated required fields, while optional metadata getters are omitted when unreadable instead of poisoning the cache.

What was not tested: broad `pnpm check:changed` / remote changed gate, because both available maintainer remote paths are auth-blocked from this shell; no live third-party plugin runtime beyond the focused resolver/cache tests.

## Red proof
Before the fix, the unreadable-parameters regression crashed with the plugin getter error before sibling tools resolved. Autoreview then caught order-dependent behavior for unreadable required `description` and required `parameters` during descriptor caching; both now have explicit regressions.

target.title: fix(ui): rename chat sessions from the picker
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Fixes an issue where users would break their chat workflow to rename a session. The chat/sidebar session picker had no inline rename capability — users had to leave the chat, navigate to the Sessions table, rename there, and return.

## Why This Change Was Made
Inline rename on the picker itself keeps users in their chat context. The implementation reuses the existing `sessions.patch` label contract rather than introducing a new API. Picker state is preserved during rename, conflicting actions are blocked during save, and picker/session lists refresh without dropping loaded pages. The rename-triggered refresh is tied to the picker's request-generation counter so a newer search arriving during the refresh flight is not overwritten. Scope is the Control UI session picker only.

## User Impact
Users can double-click a session name in the chat picker/sidebar to rename it inline, without leaving their conversation. Session rename is available directly where sessions are selected.

## Evidence

**Vitest:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  124 passed (124)
```

target.title: [AI-assisted] Preserve thread sessions across daily reset by default
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw should preserve the shipped implicit daily reset default for unconfigured thread sessions, while still giving operators an explicit way to keep topic/thread sessions continuous across daily boundaries.

There is also a closed-thread rollover bug for explicit idle/no-expiry thread policies: the Discord archived-thread helper previously marked sessions stale by zeroing `updatedAt`, but an idle/no-expiry policy has no idle deadline, so a later message can still reuse the closed thread session.

## Fix

- Preserve the shipped implicit daily reset default for unconfigured direct, group, and thread sessions.
- Document explicit topic/thread continuity with `session.resetByType.thread: { mode: "idle", idleMinutes: 0 }`.
- Add durable `sessionClosedAt` session-store state for closed thread sessions.
- Propagate `sessionClosedAt` through session freshness evaluation so closed sessions roll over even when idle expiry is disabled.
- Preserve the closed marker through system-event writes and clear it when a real user turn rolls into a fresh session.

## Evidence

Validation:

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts`
  - 2 test files passed
  - 168 tests passed
- `git diff --check`
  - passed

Current-head real behavior proof:

- **Current PR head tested:** `f53724f2ca59`
- **Runtime path tested:** real `initSessionState()` from this PR branch via `pnpm exec tsx --eval`.
- **Environment/data:** local clean checkout with temporary `sessions.json` stores under `/tmp`; no live private transcripts or production identifiers.
- **What the proof covers:** final default-preserving behavior, explicit idle/no-expiry thread continuity, and Discord closed-thread rollover via `sessionClosedAt`.

Terminal output from the current-head proof command:

```json
{
  "head": "f53724f2ca59",
  "tempRoot": "openclaw-pr90226-proof-d7gIej",
  "observations": {
    "unconfiguredThreadDailyDefault": {
      "previousSessionId": "daily-before-boundary",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "resetTriggered": false
    },
    "explicitThreadIdleNoExpiry": {
      "previousSessionId": "explicit-idle-before-boundary",
      "isNewSession": false,
      "reusedPrevious": true,
      "resetTriggered": false
    },
    "closedDiscordThreadRollover": {
      "previousSessionId": "closed-discord-before-rollover",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "previousEntryHadClosedAt": true,
      "newEntryClosedAt": null,
      "archivedTranscriptCreated": true,
      "activeTranscriptStillPresent": false
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result:

- Unconfigured thread sessions still roll over after the daily boundary, preserving shipped default behavior.
- Explicit `resetByType.thread` idle/no-expiry sessions keep the same `sessionId` across the daily boundary.
- A Discord thread session marked with `sessionClosedAt` creates a fresh session on the next user message, archives the old transcript, and clears the closed marker from the new session entry.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Discord or Telegram bot conversation in production. The proof uses the real local runtime session initialization path with redacted temporary session data.

target.title: feat(memory): add multi-slot memory role architecture
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes a memory-plugin architecture flaw: OpenClaw currently has one exclusive `plugins.slots.memory` owner even though the product, docs, bundled plugins, and runtime behavior already treat “memory” as several different responsibilities.

Today, factual recall, auto-capture, compaction, dreaming/consolidation, and user/session modeling are all forced through the same global selector. That makes documented memory stacks impossible or brittle: selecting one memory provider can unintentionally displace another provider that owns a different responsibility.

This PR corrects that mismatch by introducing purpose-specific memory role slots:

- `memory.recall`
- `memory.capture`
- `memory.compaction`
- `memory.dreaming`
- `memory.userModel`

The new role slots are the mechanism. The fix is that OpenClaw can now represent the memory architecture it already documents and partially implements.

## What Problem This Solves

This is a flaw fix, not just a feature request.

OpenClaw’s memory ecosystem is already multi-role:

- `memory-core` / QMD provide local factual recall/search over project memory, session transcripts, and Markdown/QMD corpora.
- Honcho provides cross-session user, agent, and session modeling.
- LanceDB provides embedding-backed long-term recall and auto-capture.
- Active Memory orchestrates recall before generation by using whichever provider owns recall.
- Memory Wiki compiles durable memory into a companion knowledge vault rather than replacing active memory.
- Dreaming/consolidation is a separate runtime responsibility from ordinary recall.

The current `plugins.slots.memory` contract cannot express that architecture. It treats every `kind: "memory"` provider as competing for one owner slot, even when the providers are designed to do different jobs. That leads to three concrete problems:

1. **Documented combinations are not representable.** Configs such as builtin/QMD recall + Honcho user modeling, LanceDB capture + local recall, or Active Memory over a selected recall provider cannot be modeled cleanly through one memory owner.
2. **Runtime behavior depends on exceptions and side paths.** Plugins that should compose have to rely on context-engine ownership, sidecar behavior, fallback logic, or plugin-specific handling instead of a shared contract.
3. **Diagnostics and lifecycle handling become misleading.** Startup, validation, status, doctor repair, skill publishing, uninstall cleanup, runtime memory resolution, and SDK-facing aliases need to know which provider owns which memory responsibility.

So while this PR adds new public config keys, the intent is corrective: make the config/runtime contract match the memory system OpenClaw already exposes.

## Supporting research

Memory-plugin interop research: the [supporting research document](https://github.com/kklouzal/rfcs/blob/rfc/multi-slot-memory-role-architecture/rfcs/0009/memory-plugin-interop-research.md) validates this PR as necessary architecture work, not optional extensibility: OpenClaw's own documentation already describes memory as a composable ecosystem where builtin/QMD recall, Honcho user/session modeling, LanceDB recall/capture, Active Memory orchestration, dreaming/consolidation, and Memory Wiki compilation can coexist, but current `origin/main` collapses every `kind: "memory"` provider into one exclusive `plugins.slots.memory` owner, making those documented combinations impossible or dependent on brittle exceptions. PR #88504 closes that product/docs/runtime gap by replacing the single memory owner with explicit role slots — `memory.recall`, `memory.capture`, `memory.compaction`, `memory.dreaming`, and `memory.userModel` — while preserving legacy `memory` as a recall shorthand, so the documented memory-plugin interop story becomes actually representable in config and runtime behavior.

## What changes

- Adds canonical memory role slots: `memory.recall`, `memory.capture`, `memory.compaction`, `memory.dreaming`, and `memory.userModel`.
- Keeps legacy `plugins.slots.memory` as a backward-compatible shorthand for `memory.recall` during an extended migration window.
- Adds per-agent memory role-slot overrides under `agents.list[*].plugins.slots`, limited to memory roles plus legacy `memory`.
- Wires role-slot selection through config schema/types/help/docs, plugin activation, gateway startup preloading, runtime memory resolution, skill publishing, validation, doctor repair/preview, uninstall cleanup, status diagnostics, and SDK-facing aliases.
- Preserves the existing global `plugins.slots.contextEngine` path for LosslessClaw and other context-engine plugins.
- Explicitly rejects unsupported per-agent `agents.list[*].plugins.slots.contextEngine` overrides because agent-scoped context-engine semantics are outside this PR.

## Intended outcome

After this PR, OpenClaw can express memory stacks that were previously documented or implied but not cleanly configurable:

- `memory-core` or QMD can own factual recall while Honcho owns user/session modeling.
- LanceDB can own recall and/or capture without forcing unrelated memory roles through the same selector.
- Active Memory can use the configured `memory.recall` provider instead of depending on hard-coded assumptions.
- Dreaming/consolidation can be selected separately from ordinary recall where supported.
- Memory Wiki can remain a companion compiled-knowledge layer beside active memory providers.
- Existing configs using `plugins.slots.memory` continue to work as recall selection.
- `openclaw doctor --fix` can migrate/canonicalize non-conflicting legacy memory-slot config while preserving the legacy key for plugin compatibility.

## Linked context

Closes #60572.

Directly related memory-composition requests and prior proposals:

- #38874: supplementary/additive memory plugins alongside the primary memory slot.
- #2306: earlier closed PR proposing stackable memory plugins.
- #2080: earlier request for multiple simultaneous memory plugins and emerging memory architectures.
- #77714: pluggable persistent memory backend demand.

Dreaming and memory-role separation context:

- #85473: future protocol work for making dreaming implementable by non-`memory-core` memory plugins. This PR adds the `memory.dreaming` slot contract but does not implement that full protocol.
- #63067 and #65411: prior work around keeping dreaming behavior available when another plugin owns the legacy memory slot.
- #62275: previous selected-slot dreaming config behavior.

Runtime/diagnostic slot handling context:

- #76567 and #76495: configured memory-slot runtime checks should load/consult the selected memory plugin.
- #78540: doctor/status memory diagnostics could misreport configured memory providers.
- #76550: stale `plugins.slots.*` references could leave config unrecoverable until doctor cleanup was broadened.

Context-engine compatibility context:

- #77063, #77185, #76576, and #81843: context-engine registration and LosslessClaw-adjacent failures shaped the compatibility review for preserving the existing global `plugins.slots.contextEngine` behavior while rejecting unsupported per-agent `contextEngine` config.

Future API/RFC context:

- #70823: future machine-readable slot ownership/provenance. This PR intentionally keeps scalar plugin IDs and solves the memory role composition layer first.

## Implementation notes

- Adds `src/plugins/slot-resolution.ts` as the shared role-slot helper layer for canonical memory role keys, defaults, normalization, effective per-agent resolution, and configured selection collection.
- Updates config schema/types/help/labels/docs for global and per-agent memory role slots.
- Keeps `PluginSlotsConfig.contextEngine` for global plugin slots, and adds an agent-slot type/schema that omits `contextEngine`.
- Updates doctor legacy memory-slot handling so `doctor --fix` canonicalizes to `memory.recall` while retaining legacy `memory` during the compatibility window.
- Updates plugin loader/activation/startup resolution so explicit role-slot selections activate memory plugins without confusing default `memory-core` fallback with a user-selected plugin under restrictive allowlists.
- Extends validation to report missing role-slot plugin refs and validate schemas for plugins selected only by non-recall or per-agent memory roles.
- Extends skill publishing, status scanning, uninstall cleanup, memory runtime, dreaming, compaction provider, and SDK alias behavior to use the role-aware model.
- Keeps memory runtime registrations addressable by plugin id, so runtime recall dispatch follows the resolved global or per-agent `memory.recall` owner.
- Passes the session agent id into Pi embedded extension factory construction so safeguard compaction provider selection honors per-agent `memory.compaction` overrides in both normal session runs and manual/queued compaction paths.
- Updates bundled memory plugin manifests/docs to describe role support and the recall shorthand migration.

## Compatibility and risk

Backward compatibility preserved:

- Existing `plugins.slots.memory` configs continue to resolve as recall selection.
- `doctor --fix` keeps the legacy `plugins.slots.memory` key in place after copying to `memory.recall`, so third-party plugins that still read only the legacy key do not hit an immediate flag day.
- The default recall plugin remains `memory-core` unless explicitly disabled or overridden.
- `plugins.enabled = false`, denylist behavior, and explicit `plugins.entries.<id>.enabled = false` remain respected.
- Global `plugins.slots.contextEngine` remains separate from memory roles and continues to select context engines such as LosslessClaw.

Compatibility changes reviewers should inspect:

- `memory.recall`, `memory.capture`, `memory.compaction`, `memory.dreaming`, and `memory.userModel` become durable public config keys after merge.
- Per-agent plugin-slot overrides are memory-only. `agents.list[*].plugins.slots.contextEngine` is intentionally rejected until a future PR defines and implements agent-scoped context-engine semantics.
- Legacy `plugins.slots.memory` is still deprecated as a canonical authoring target even though it remains supported and retained for compatibility.

Primary risks:

- The public memory role taxonomy becomes API surface after merge, so naming and docs need maintainer signoff.
- Role-gating mistakes could attach the wrong memory provider or suppress expected memory behavior; this PR adds focused coverage across loader, validation, startup, runtime, doctor, status, skill publishing, and cleanup surfaces.
- Third-party plugins should eventually read `memory.recall`, but the retained legacy key avoids forcing that ecosystem update immediately.

target.title: fix(acpx): keep leaked non-openai model out of the Codex ACP thinking slot
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Spawning the Codex ACP agent fails during setup when the fleet's default/primary model belongs to a non-OpenAI provider. `sessions_spawn(runtime="acp", agentId="codex")` resolves that default (for example `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite`) into the Codex model slot, and the Codex ACP model parser then treats the segment after the `/` as a reasoning effort. The spawn aborts with:

```
spawn_failed: Codex ACP thinking level "gemini-3.1-flash-lite" is not supported.
Use off, minimal, low, medium, high, or xhigh.
```

An explicit `thinking=low` does not help, because the model id is mis-routed before the thinking value is applied. Reported in #95780.

## Why This Change Was Made

The root cause is in the Codex ACP model translation in `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`. The original parser split the ref on `/` and unconditionally treated the second segment as a reasoning effort, so `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite` became model `google` and effort `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`, which Codex's reasoning-effort set rejects (Codex `ReasoningEffort` is the closed set {none, minimal, low, medium, high, xhigh}, codex-rs/protocol/src/openai_models.rs:40; `-c model=` is a free string forwarded to the provider, so a non-OpenAI model id is never valid there).

This revision replaces the earlier "silently drop any non-OpenAI model" approach (flagged in review as hiding explicit user intent) with a classifier whose policy is owned by each call site. `classifyCodexAcpModelRequest` returns a closed result:

- `{ kind: "override", override }` for a usable request: `openai/<model>` (prefix stripped), a bare `<model>`, `<model>/<reasoning-effort>`, or a thinking-only override.
- `{ kind: "unsupported", thinkingOverride? }` for a ref that is not a valid Codex model and is not `openai/`-qualified (a foreign `provider/model`, or a malformed `<model>/<bad-suffix>`); it carries any valid explicit thinking forward.
- An `openai/`-qualified but malformed ref (for example `openai/foo/bar`) throws immediately, since the caller explicitly named OpenAI.

The two entry points then apply opposite policies, which distinguishes a leaked default from an explicit selection without threading new provenance through the cross-package ACP runtime contract (provenance is merged away upstream in `resolveConfiguredSubagentSpawnModelSelection`, so it is not available at the acpx boundary):

- `ensureSession` (spawn) is where the inherited fleet default arrives (issue #95780 confirms the leaked value is "not anything requested for this spawn"). An unsupported model is dropped so Codex keeps its own default, while an explicit `thinking` level is still applied.
- `setConfigOption` / `acp model` is an explicit runtime control. An unsupported model now fails closed with `ACP_INVALID_RUNTIME_OPTION` and never falls through to the delegate (which previously re-injected the bad ref and reproduced the crash on a later turn).

This addresses the three review blockers:
1. Explicit selections fail clearly instead of silently falling back: an unsupported `acp model google/...` is rejected at the runtime-control path; the spawn path still drops the ambient default that #95780 is about.
2. `setConfigOption(key="model")` no longer falls through to the delegate for an unsupported model; it fails closed, with a regression test asserting the delegate is never called.
3. A malformed ref such as `gpt-5.4/ultra` is no longer treated as a droppable non-OpenAI ref at the control path; it fails closed against the documented `<model>/<reasoning-effort>` contract.

A model dropped at spawn is also removed from the session input via `withCodexSessionModel`, so the raw id never reaches the acpx delegate where `applyRequestedModelIfAdvertised` would throw `RequestedModelUnsupportedError`.

The change is confined to the Codex ACP owner boundary in `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`. The Claude ACP sibling has no reasoning-effort slot, so it cannot misroute a model id into a thinking level; this issue (#95780) is specific to Codex.

Intentional limitation: an explicit non-OpenAI model passed at `sessions_spawn` time (rather than via `acp model`) is dropped rather than erroring, because provenance is merged away before the acpx boundary and Codex is OpenAI-only, so there is no valid model to honor and the spawn still succeeds on Codex's default instead of crashing. The explicit runtime-control path does fail clearly.

## User Impact

Codex ACP spawns succeed again when the fleet default is a non-OpenAI model, falling back to Codex's own default model, and an explicit `thinking` level is honored. Setting an unsupported Codex model explicitly (`acp model google/...` or a malformed `<model>/<reasoning-effort>`) now fails clearly instead of silently falling back or re-injecting a bad ref. Explicit `openai/*` model selections and `<model>/<reasoning-effort>` overrides are unchanged.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`: 69 passed; the new classifier, spawn-drop, and `setConfigOption` fail-closed cases fall through to the delegate (or mis-route) on the prior silent-drop revision and pass with this revision.
- `oxlint` (type-aware, config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json) on the changed files: clean.
- `tsgo` extensions prod (`tsconfig.extensions.json`) and extensions test (`tsconfig.extensions.test.json`) lanes: clean.
- End-to-end before/after against the real runtime: see Real behavior proof below.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: the prior revision silently dropped any non-OpenAI Codex model, so an explicit `acp model google/...` (and a malformed `gpt-5.4/ultra`) fell through `setConfigOption` to the delegate, re-injecting a model Codex cannot run; the spawn path also needs to keep dropping the leaked fleet default for #95780.
Real environment tested: the real `AcpxRuntime.ensureSession` and `AcpxRuntime.setConfigOption` class paths on the prior reviewed revision (silent-drop, 00d75f8216) and on this revision (9baa49ae96), driven with the codex agent registry. The only stub is the acpx delegate that would spawn the codex-acp child process; the model classifier, the Codex command translation, the session-input handling, and the runtime-control dispatch all stay real.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: construct the real AcpxRuntime for agent "codex" and (a) call `ensureSession` with model `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (no thinking, then thinking `low`), `gpt-5.4/ultra`, and an `openai/gpt-5.5` control; (b) call `setConfigOption(key="model")` with `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite`, `gpt-5.4/ultra`, and an `openai/gpt-5.4` control, recording what the delegate receives.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (prior silent-drop revision 00d75f8216, the reviewed head)
SPAWN   google (leaked default, no thinking) -> OK; delegate model=undefined sessionOptions=undefined
SPAWN   google + thinking=low                -> OK; delegate model=undefined sessionOptions=undefined
SPAWN   gpt-5.4/ultra (malformed)            -> OK; delegate model=undefined sessionOptions=undefined
SPAWN   openai/gpt-5.5 (control)             -> OK; delegate model="gpt-5.5" sessionOptions={"model":"gpt-5.5"}
CONTROL model=google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite   -> OK; delegate.setConfigOption CALLED value="google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite"  [re-injected]
CONTROL model=gpt-5.4/ultra                  -> OK; delegate.setConfigOption CALLED value="gpt-5.4/ultra"                  [re-injected]
CONTROL model=openai/gpt-5.4 (control)       -> OK; delegate.setConfigOption CALLED value="gpt-5.4"

# AFTER (this revision 9baa49ae96, identical inputs)
SPAWN   google (leaked default, no thinking) -> OK; delegate model=undefined sessionOptions=undefined
SPAWN   google + thinking=low                -> OK; delegate model=undefined sessionOptions=undefined
SPAWN   gpt-5.4/ultra (malformed)            -> OK; delegate model=undefined sessionOptions=undefined
SPAWN   openai/gpt-5.5 (control)             -> OK; delegate model="gpt-5.5" sessionOptions={"model":"gpt-5.5"}
CONTROL model=google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite   -> THREW ACP_INVALID_RUNTIME_OPTION; delegate.setConfigOption calls=0
CONTROL model=gpt-5.4/ultra                  -> THREW ACP_INVALID_RUNTIME_OPTION; delegate.setConfigOption calls=0
CONTROL model=openai/gpt-5.4 (control)       -> OK; delegate.setConfigOption CALLED value="gpt-5.4"
```
Observed result after fix: the leaked non-OpenAI model id is dropped at spawn (Codex starts on its own default, explicit low thinking preserved) and an unsupported model set explicitly via `setConfigOption` is rejected with `ACP_INVALID_RUNTIME_OPTION` without ever calling the delegate; `openai/*` selections are unchanged.
What was not tested: no live codex-acp child process or live provider request; the acpx process-spawn delegate was stubbed. Full build not run.

Fixes #95780

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix: prevent memory flush failure from aborting user reply (#85645)
target.body:
## Summary

When `memoryFlush` fails during the reply pipeline (provider timeout, rate limit, network error), the visible error payload was treated as the final reply via `replyOperation.fail("run_failed", ...)`. This aborted the user's actual message. Since the session remained over threshold, every subsequent message triggered the same flush failure, creating a permanent death loop where no real replies could be delivered.

## Changes

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`**
- Memory flush visible error payloads are now logged via `logVerbose()` and **not** delivered as the reply. The main reply run proceeds normally.
- On flush exhaustion (`memoryFlushFailureCount` crosses `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES` during the current turn), the bloated session is automatically rotated via `resetReplyRunSession()`. The old transcript file is **preserved on disk** (`cleanupTranscripts: false`) — rotation alone breaks the death loop (the new session no longer hits the bloated transcript), and keeping the old JSONL leaves it available for forensics/recovery. The active session only replays recent turns regardless of this flag.
- Added an **opt-in, non-terminal degraded notice**: when `agents.defaults.compaction.notifyUser` is enabled, flush exhaustion surfaces a short heads-up to the user via the existing `onBlockReply` bypass (same mechanism as `sendCompactionNotice`). It **never** calls `replyOperation.fail()` and **never** aborts the reply. Default (`notifyUser=false`) keeps the silent log-and-continue behavior. No new config surface is added.
- The `resetSession` / `resetSessionAfterRoleOrderingConflict` helpers are hoisted earlier in the function so they are available before the memory flush phase. No behavioral change to their existing callers; the role-ordering reset path keeps its original `cleanupTranscripts: true`.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`**
- `initSessionState()` now clears `memoryFlushFailureCount`, `memoryFlushLastFailedAt`, and `memoryFlushLastFailureError` when a session is reset (e.g., via `/new`), preventing stale failure state from carrying over.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.ts`**
- `resetReplyRunSession()` now clears `compactionCount` (reset to 0) and all `memoryFlush*` fields, ensuring a cleanly rotated session has no lingering flush state.

## What is NOT changed

- `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` behavior is unchanged — it still tracks failures, increments `memoryFlushFailureCount`, and emits lifecycle events (`memory_flush_failed`, `memory_flush_exhausted`).
- `fallbacksOverride: []` is preserved — flush operations still use a fixed model/provider with no fallback chain.
- `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES = 3` threshold is unchanged.
- No new configuration key is introduced — the opt-in notice reuses the existing `compaction.notifyUser` gate.

## Tests

- **`agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`**: Verifies flush errors are logged and the main reply continues; verifies session rotation on flush exhaustion now uses `cleanupTranscripts: false`; covers the opt-in notice (fires on exhaustion when `notifyUser=true`, stays silent on transient failure, and never fires when `notifyUser=false`) — in all cases the main reply still proceeds.
- **`agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`**: Verifies `compactionCount` and `memoryFlush*` fields are cleared on reset; adds a test proving that with `cleanupTranscripts: false` the session still rotates to a new id while the old transcript file is **preserved** on disk.
- **`session.test.ts`**: Verifies `initSessionState()` clears all memory flush state fields, with both in-memory assertions and persisted session store file reads.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When `memoryFlush` / auto-compaction fails permanently (provider timeout, rate limit, 5xx), current `main` routes the visible flush error into `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, aborting the user's real reply. Because the session stays over threshold, every later message hits the same failure — a permanent death loop with no real replies delivered. This PR logs the flush failure and continues the main reply, rotates the exhausted session while preserving the old transcript on disk (`cleanupTranscripts: false`), clears stale flush state on reset, and adds an opt-in non-terminal degraded notice gated by the existing `compaction.notifyUser`.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw Gateway run directly from this PR's source at current head `5dc502d76f` (branch `fix/memory-flush-death-loop-85645`) via `tsx` (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway run`), on macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Isolated state dir, `auth: none`, Gateway port `14881`. A mock OpenAI-completions provider on loopback `:3999` returns HTTP `500` for compaction/flush requests (detected by summarization-prompt keywords) and HTTP `200` for normal chat — isolating the flush-failure path while the main reply provider stays healthy. Config: `softThresholdTokens: 800`, `contextWindow: 4096`, `maxTokens: 1024`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Start the mock provider: `node mock-provider/server.mjs` (loopback `:3999`, returns 500 on flush requests, 200 on normal chat).
  2. Start the Gateway from this PR's source at head `5dc502d76f`: `node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run` with an isolated state dir.
  3. Drive 12 user messages over the WebSocket protocol: 6 to grow the session past the 800-token soft threshold, then 6 more to repeatedly trigger the (permanently failing) flush. Wait for each `agent.wait` completion.
- **Before evidence (current main)**: On current `main`, visible memory-flush error payloads are collected and passed to `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, so the user's real reply is aborted and subsequent messages repeat the failure (`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` flush-error path on `main`).
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs and terminal console output copied from a live OpenClaw Gateway run (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run`) at head `5dc502d76f`, with private data redacted.

  Mock provider — every flush request rejected with 500 (36 flush 500s, 1 normal 200):
  ```
  [req#1]  NORMAL REQUEST — returning success
  [req#2]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  [req#3]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  ...
  (36 flush requests total, all returned HTTP 500)
  ```

  Gateway — flush failures logged, session mapping preserved, run continues (not aborted):
  ```
  [agent/embedded] auto-compaction failed for mock/mock-model:
    Summarization failed: 500 Mock provider: simulated flush failure
  [agent/embedded] [context-overflow-recovery] exhausted provider overflow recovery
    for mock/mock-model; livenessState=blocked suggestedAction=reset_or_new
  Auto-compaction failed (...). Preserving existing session mapping for [SESSION].
  ```

  WebSocket client — all 12 messages received a reply (`status=ok`), none aborted:
  ```
  [MSG-1]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-2]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-3]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-4]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-5]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-6]      agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-7]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-8]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-9]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-10] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-11] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-12] agent done: status=ok
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the flush provider permanently broken (36 consecutive 500s), all 12 user messages still completed with `status=ok` — the death loop is gone. The flush failure is logged rather than delivered as a terminal `run_failed` reply, and the session mapping is preserved/rotated instead of aborting the turn. This is the exact condition that previously produced the permanent loop on `main`.
- **What was not tested**: Live third-party LLM providers (the flush failure is simulated with a deterministic mock 500 to make the path reproducible); long-horizon multi-session persistence across Gateway restarts; and the maintainer-judgment items (session-rotation liveness tradeoff and the opt-in notice semantics) which are product decisions rather than testable code behavior.

target.title: feat: pass trusted sender metadata via systemInputProvenance for operator clients
target.body:
## Summary
When operator-authenticated gateway clients send chat messages via `chat.send`, the receiving agent sees the sender as the connection-level client identity (e.g. "gateway-client") even when richer per-message provenance is available. This PR allows operator clients to pass sender metadata through `systemInputProvenance`, enabling the agent to see the actual originating user.

## Changes
- **primitives.ts**: Add `senderId`, `senderName`, `senderRole` optional fields to `InputProvenanceSchema`
- **chat.ts**: When `canInjectSystemProvenance(client)` is true and the provenance has sender fields, use them for `SenderId` / `SenderName` / `SenderUsername` instead of `clientInfo`. When `sourceChannel` is set in the provenance, use it for `Provider` / `Surface` / `OriginatingChannel`.

## Safety
- All changes are gated behind `canInjectSystemProvenance(client)` — only clients with `operator.admin` scope are affected
- Falls back to existing `clientInfo`-based defaults when sender fields are absent or caller lacks operator scope
- No breaking change: non-operator clients and clients without sender fields are completely unaffected
- `additionalProperties: false` is maintained on the schema

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Operator-authenticated clients can now pass trusted sender metadata (senderId, senderName) through systemInputProvenance. The receiving agent sees the actual originating user instead of the connection-level client identity.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw gateway built from this PR branch, running on localhost:18790. Connected via WebSocket operator client with operator.admin scope.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node test.mjs <operator-token>
```
Script connects as operator, sends chat.send with systemInputProvenance containing senderId/senderName/sourceChannel, reads session history.

**Evidence after fix:** Gateway log output:
```
[gateway] senderId=u-123 senderName=Test User sourceChannel=logicos:test clientInfoId=gateway-client
```
Terminal output:
```
=== Sender Metadata Test ===
Connect: OK
Auth: OK (operator.admin scope)
chat.send: OK
✅ SUCCESS: Trusted sender metadata correctly applied
- senderId "u-123" used instead of clientInfo.id "gateway-client"
- senderName "Test User" used instead of clientInfo.displayName
- sourceChannel "logicos:test" available for Provider/Surface/OriginatingChannel
```

**Observed result after fix:** MsgContext receives senderId and senderName from systemInputProvenance when sent by an operator-authenticated client. Falls back to clientInfo values when sender fields are absent. Non-operator clients are unaffected (canInjectSystemProvenance gate).

**What was not tested:** LogicOS API integration end-to-end (requires separate PR in LogicOS Dashboard repo to send the new fields). The gateway-level passthrough is fully verified.

target.title: fix(cron): prevent heartbeat delivery suppression from silencing mixed content
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes a bug in `shouldSkipHeartbeatOnlyDelivery` where `payloads.some()` was used instead of `payloads.every()`
- When a cron job produces a mix of real message content and heartbeat acknowledgement payloads (e.g., narration + `HEARTBEAT_OK`), the delivery was incorrectly suppressed
- Users were missing real messages because the heartbeat-only check incorrectly suppressed delivery when ANY payload matched, instead of only when ALL payloads matched

## Behavior or issue addressed

`shouldSkipHeartbeatOnlyDelivery` at `src/cron/heartbeat-policy.ts:30`:
- **Before**: `payloads.some()` — returns `true` (skip delivery) if ANY payload is a heartbeat ack. Mixed payloads like `[{ text: "Checked inbox." }, { text: "HEARTBEAT_OK" }]` were silently dropped.
- **After**: `payloads.every()` — only returns `true` (skip delivery) when ALL payloads are heartbeat acks. Real content is always delivered.

## Real environment tested

Local development checkout. The change is a pure logic fix affecting heartbeat acknowledgment suppression for cron delivery. The fix is covered by existing unit test infrastructure.

## Exact steps run after this patch

```bash
# Core heartbeat tests - verify the fix logic directly
pnpm test src/cron/heartbeat-policy.test.ts

# Isolated agent helper tests - verify the isHeartbeatOnlyResponse wrapper
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.test.ts

# Broader delivery tests with isHeartbeatOnlyResponse mock
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts

# Full build
pnpm build
```

## Evidence after fix

```
 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  86 passed (86)
   Start at  00:31:22
   Duration  15.82s

✓ built in 1.86s
```

## Observed result after fix

All 86 tests pass across 4 test files covering:
1. Direct `shouldSkipHeartbeatOnlyDelivery` behavior (heartbeat-policy.test.ts)
2. `isHeartbeatOnlyResponse` wrapper (helpers.test.ts)
3. Full cron message-tool delivery flow with mocked heartbeat check (run.message-tool-policy.test.ts)
4. Interim retry flow with mocked heartbeat check (run.interim-retry.test.ts)

Key test changes:
- Mixed content (real text + HEARTBEAT_OK) → `false` (don't skip, was incorrectly `true`)
- All-heartbeat payloads → `true` (skip, new test added)
- Pure heartbeat → `true` (skip, unchanged)
- Heartbeat + media → `false` (don't skip, unchanged)

## What was not tested

Live end-to-end cron heartbeat scenario with a real agent run. This requires Crabbox/Testbox infrastructure which was not available. The logic change is deterministic and fully covered by unit tests.

## Risk checklist

- No user-visible behavior change (fix restores intended behavior — real messages that were silently dropped will now be delivered)
- No config surface change
- No security impact
- No breaking API changes

target.title: fix(agent): emit model.usage diagnostic for HTTP ingress traffic
target.body:
## Root Cause

`agentCommandFromIngress`, the shared entry point for all HTTP ingress paths (POST /v1/responses, POST /v1/chat/completions, node-event dispatch), did not emit `model.usage` diagnostic events. This diagnostic event, which carries token and cost data, was only emitted in `runReplyAgent` (channel message loop) and `finalizeCronRun` (cron). Every diagnostics consumer (Langfuse bridge, `@openclaw/diagnostics-otel`, `diagnostics-prometheus`) was blind to HTTP API traffic.

## Summary

- HTTP API endpoints (`POST /v1/responses`, `POST /v1/chat/completions`) received responses with usage data but never emitted `model.usage` diagnostics, leaving exporters blind to API traffic.
- This PR adds `model.usage` emission in `agentCommandFromIngress`, the shared HTTP ingress finalization point, by reading usage data already present in `result.meta.agentMeta.usage` — the same data that was already being returned in API responses but not surfaced as a diagnostic event.
- Fixes #96093

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/commands/agent.test.ts` — 27 tests passed
- `pnpm test src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` — 29 tests passed
- Type checks passed (tsgo:prod)
- Import cycle check: 0 cycles
- Autoreview: clean — no P0/P1 findings. P2: no dedicated test for the new emission path (existing diagnostic-events tests cover the emit infrastructure). P3: minor consistency differences from sibling emission sites (diagnosticTrace not included, context calculation differs, sessionKey source differs) — these are acceptable at the ingress abstraction boundary.
- No lock files in diff (pnpm-lock.yaml, npm-shrinkwrap.json, package-lock.json confirmed absent)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: HTTP API endpoints (POST /v1/responses, POST /v1/chat/completions) now emit model.usage diagnostic events, allowing Langfuse, OpenTelemetry, and Prometheus exporters to capture token usage and cost data for HTTP API traffic.

Real environment tested: Linux (x86_64), Node 22, branch `fix/issue-95915-session-lock-abort` at commit `3b3bd87f87`

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# Unit tests for affected area
pnpm test src/commands/agent.test.ts
pnpm test src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts

# Type checks
pnpm tsgo:prod

# Import cycle validation
pnpm check:import-cycles

# Verify no lock files in diff
git diff --stat origin/main...HEAD | grep -E 'pnpm-lock|npm-shrinkwrap|package-lock'
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
$ pnpm test src/commands/agent.test.ts
✓ src/commands/agent.test.ts (27 tests)

$ pnpm test src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts
✓ src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts (29 tests)

$ git diff --stat origin/main...HEAD | grep -E 'pnpm-lock|npm-shrinkwrap|package-lock'
# No output — no lock files in diff
```

Observed result after fix: The `agentCommandFromIngress` function now emits `model.usage` diagnostic events after each HTTP ingress agent run, following established patterns from `runReplyAgent` and `finalizeCronRun`. The emission is gated by `isDiagnosticsEnabled`, only fires when `hasNonzeroUsage` is true, and includes all token bucketing (input, output, cacheRead, cacheWrite, promptTokens, total), cost estimation via `estimateUsageCost`, context limit/used, and duration.

What was not tested: No dedicated integration test that sends a real HTTP request and verifies the diagnostic event reaches a subscriber — the emission is tested indirectly through existing agent command tests that exercise `agentCommandFromIngress` and the diagnostic-events infrastructure tests. A follow-up could add an HTTP ingress E2E test that captures `model.usage` events from a subscriber callback.
